- `onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldVersion, int newVersion)` → called when version number increases. You write `ALTER TABLE` / `DROP` logic here.

### 2.1.3 CRUD Workflow (Why → How)

| Step | Operation | SQL Command | Why It Matters |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | Create DB | `getWritableDatabase()` | Triggers `onCreate` |
| 2 | Insert | `INSERT INTO student(name, marks) VALUES(?, ?)` | Persists a new row |
| 3 | Read | `SELECT * FROM student WHERE id = ?` | Returns a `Cursor` object |
| 4 | Update | `UPDATE student SET marks = ? WHERE id = ?` | Modifies an existing row |
| 5 | Delete | `DELETE FROM student WHERE id = ?` | Removes a row |

### 2.1.4 The `Cursor` Concept

A `Cursor` is a **pointer to a result set** of rows returned by a query. It is NOT a list. You must call `cursor.moveToNext()` in a loop and read columns via `cursor.getString(0)`, `cursor.getInt(1)`, etc. Always close it in a `finally` block to prevent memory leaks.

---

## 2.2 Realm — The Object-Oriented Contender

### 2.2.1 Why Realm Was Invented

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The traditional SQL + ORM (like Room) approach requires **mapping Java/Kotlin objects to SQL rows and back again** — a process that creates a *parity impedance mismatch*. Realm eliminates this by storing the **actual object graph natively**, without SQL.

### 2.2.2 Theoretical Model

- A class extending `RealmObject` becomes a persistable entity.
- A `Realm` instance is your handle to the database (similar to a `SQLiteDatabase`).
- Writes happen inside a `realm.executeTransaction { ... }` block (transactional, ACID).
- Reads can be done live (auto-updating) or snapshot.

### 2.2.3 Realm Object Lifecycle

1. **Create** → `realm.createObject(Student::class.java, primaryKey)`
2. **Query** → `realm.where(Student::class.java).equalTo("branch", "CSE").findAll()`
3. **Update** → Mutate the object inside a transaction; changes auto-persist.
4. **Delete** → `student.deleteFromRealm()` inside a transaction.

### 2.2.4 Realm vs Room (KTU Common Confusion)

| Property | Realm | Room (SQLite ORM) |
|----------|-------|-------------------|
| Language | Pure Object graph | SQL + Annotations |
| Speed | **2–10× faster** for large datasets | Adequate for small data |
| Schema migration | Mostly automatic | Manual via `Migration` class |
| Live data | Built-in `RealmChangeListener` | Manual via `LiveData` / `Flow` |

---

## 2.3 Firebase Realtime Database (RTDB) — The Cloud Synced NoSQL

### 2.3.1 Theoretical Model

Firebase RTDB stores data as a **single巨大的 JSON tree**. Every node is a key-value pair, and values can be strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, or nested objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example JSON Tree Structure:**
> ```json
> {
>   "users": {
>     "uid_alice": { "name": "Alice", "score": 90 },
>     "uid_bob":   { "name": "Bob",   "score": 75 }
>   }
> }
> ```

### 2.3.2 Core Concepts (Why Each Exists)

| Concept | Why It Exists |
|---------|---------------|
| `DatabaseReference` | A pointer to a node in the JSON tree |
| `ValueEventListener` | Fires on data change (real-time push) |
| `addValueEventListener` | Continuous sync (live updates) |
| `addListenerForSingleValueEvent` | One-time read |
| `onDisconnect()` | Server remembers a client's state when it goes offline |
| `.push()` | Generates a unique timestamp-based key for new child |
| **Offline Support** | Local cache enabled via `FirebaseDatabase.getInstance().setPersistenceEnabled(true)` |

### 2.3.3 How Real-Time Push Works (The Engineering)

1. Client A writes `score: 95` to `/users/uid_alice/`.
2. Firebase cloud server receives the write, validates security rules, and commits.
3. Firebase maintains a **persistent WebSocket** to every connected client.
4. Client B (subscribed to that path) receives the update within ~100ms.
5. Local cache on Client B is updated → UI re-renders via listener callback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is fundamentally different from polling (which would hammer the server with HTTP requests every second). The WebSocket is a long-lived bidirectional TCP connection.

### 2.3.4 Security Rules (The Gatekeeper)

Firebase RTDB uses a JSON-based DSL for security:

```json
{
  "rules": {
    "users": {
      "$uid": {
        ".read": "auth != null && auth.uid === $uid",
        ".write": "auth != null && auth.uid === $uid"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

> This means: a user can read/write ONLY their own data node.

---

## 2.4 KTU 2024 Comparison Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most important table for KTU Module 3 exam answers.

| Criterion | SQLite | Realm | Firebase RTDB |
|-----------|--------|-------|---------------|
| **Type** | Relational SQL | Object DB | Cloud NoSQL |
| **Storage** | Local file | Local file + optional sync | Cloud + local cache |
| **Schema** | Fixed schema required | Class-based (loose) | Schemaless JSON |
| **Query Language** | SQL | Realm Query Language (RQL) | Path-based + `orderByChild` |
| **Real-time Sync** | ❌ No | ⚠️ Via Realm Sync (paid) | ✅ Built-in WebSocket |
| **Offline Support** | ✅ (it is offline by default) | ✅ (offline by default) | ✅ (with `setPersistenceEnabled`) |
| **Speed for 10K rows** | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Multi-user collaboration** | ❌ Manual | ❌ Manual | ✅ Native |
| **Cost** | Free | Free (Sync is paid) | Free tier + Blaze plan |
| **Best For** | Offline-first, structured data | Object-heavy apps, performance | Chat, live tracking, IoT |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where each database shines in industry:**
> - **SQLite** → Built-in to every Android phone. Used by Android OS itself (Contacts, SMS metadata), WhatsApp message store, browser bookmarks.
> - **Realm** → Used by Starbucks, eBay, Nike for offline-first catalog browsing.
> - **Firebase RTDB** → Used by Uber (driver location), Google Docs collab, gaming leaderboards, chat apps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a CS/IT course (Mobile App Development), the KTU board permits implementation code. Below are exhaustive, line-by-line walkthroughs that mirror a typical KTU "Part B" 14-mark question structure.

## 3.1 SQLite: Full CRUD Implementation (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Define the Helper Class

```java
public class DBHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper {
    private static final String DB_NAME   = "students.db";
    private static final int    DB_VERSION = 1;

    public DBHelper(Context ctx) {
        super(ctx, DB_NAME, null, DB_VERSION);
    }

    @Override
    public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) {
        // First-time DB creation logic
        String sql = "CREATE TABLE student ("
                   + "id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, "
                   + "name TEXT, "
                   + "marks INTEGER)";
        db.execSQL(sql);
    }

    @Override
    public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldV, int newV) {
        db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS student");
        onCreate(db);
    }
}
```

**Conversion Logic (line-by-line):**
- `SQLiteOpenHelper` is an abstract class. We **must** extend it and provide the two callbacks.
- `DB_NAME` → the physical file the OS will create.
- `DB_VERSION` → increment this whenever schema changes; triggers `onUpgrade`.
- `onCreate` runs ONCE — the perfect place for `CREATE TABLE`.
- `onUpgrade` is the only safe place for `DROP TABLE` to avoid corruption.

### Step 2 — Insert (Create)

```java
SQLiteDatabase db = helper.getWritableDatabase();
ContentValues cv = new ContentValues();
cv.put("name", "Alice");
cv.put("marks", 92);
long rowId = db.insert("student", null, cv);
```

**Why this works:** `getWritableDatabase()` opens the DB file with read+write permissions, which internally triggers `onCreate` if the file is absent. `ContentValues` is a key-value map that maps column names to values — safer than string concatenation (which is vulnerable to SQL injection).

### Step 3 — Read (Query)

```java
SQLiteDatabase db = helper.getReadableDatabase();
Cursor c = db.rawQuery("SELECT * FROM student WHERE marks > ?", new String[]{"80"});
if (c.moveToFirst()) {
    do {
        String name  = c.getString(c.getColumnIndexOrThrow("name"));
        int    marks = c.getInt(c.getColumnIndexOrThrow("marks"));
        Log.d("ROW", name + " → " + marks);
    } while (c.moveToNext());
}
c.close();
```

**Why this works:** `Cursor` acts like a row pointer. `moveToFirst()` positions the cursor at the first row (returns `false` if empty). `getColumnIndexOrThrow` is the safe way to look up a column index by name.

### Step 4 — Update

```java
ContentValues cv = new ContentValues();
cv.put("marks", 95);
int rows = db.update("student", cv, "id = ?", new String[]{"1"});
```

### Step 5 — Delete

```java
int rows = db.delete("student", "marks < ?", new String[]{"50"});
```

---

## 3.2 Realm: Object-Oriented CRUD (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Add Gradle Dependency

```gradle
implementation 'io.realm:realm-android:10.18.0'
```

### Step 2 — Define the RealmObject

```java
public class Student extends RealmObject {
    @PrimaryKey
    private int    id;
    private String name;
    private int    marks;
    // getters and setters...
}
```

> **Why this is powerful:** No `CREATE TABLE` SQL is ever written. The class itself **is** the schema. Realm reflects on the class and creates the file structure at runtime.

### Step 3 — Initialize Realm

```java
Realm.init(context);
RealmConfiguration config = new RealmConfiguration.Builder().build();
Realm realm = Realm.getInstance(config);
```

### Step 4 — Insert Inside a Transaction

```java
realm.executeTransaction(r -> {
    Student s = r.createObject(Student.class, 1);
    s.setName("Alice");
    s.setMarks(92);
});
```

**Conversion Logic:** Realm writes are always transactional. The lambda runs in a write-locked context. If it throws, the transaction auto-rolls back.

### Step 5 — Query (No SQL, Uses RQL)

```java
RealmResults<Student> results = realm.where(Student.class)
                                     .greaterThan("marks", 80)
                                     .findAll();
results.addChangeListener(students -> updateUI(students));
```

**Why `addChangeListener` matters:** The `RealmResults` object is **live** — it auto-updates the moment any matching object changes. No manual refresh needed.

### Step 6 — Delete

```java
realm.executeTransaction(r -> {
    Student s = r.where(Student.class).equalTo("id", 1).findFirst();
    if (s != null) s.deleteFromRealm();
});
```

---

## 3.3 Firebase Realtime Database: Real-Time Sync (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Add Dependency and Initialize

```gradle
implementation 'com.google.firebase:firebase-database:20.1.0'
```

In `Application` class:
```java
FirebaseDatabase.getInstance().setPersistenceEnabled(true);
```

> **Why:** Enables offline caching. When the user is offline, writes are queued and synced automatically when the connection returns.

### Step 2 — Get a Reference

```java
DatabaseReference ref = FirebaseDatabase.getInstance()
                                         .getReference("users")
                                         .child("uid_alice");
```

### Step 3 — Write Data

```java
ref.child("score").setValue(95);
```

### Step 4 — Read with a Live Listener

```java
ref.addValueEventListener(new ValueEventListener() {
    @Override
    public void onDataChange(DataSnapshot snap) {
        int score = snap.child("score").getValue(Integer.class);
        textView.setText("Score: " + score);
    }
    @Override
    public void onCancelled(DatabaseError error) {
        Log.e("FB", error.getMessage());
    }
});
```

**Why `onDataChange` keeps firing:** The WebSocket connection is persistent. Any server-side change triggers this callback — even if the change was made by another user on another continent.

### Step 5 — Push a New Child with Auto-Generated Key

```java
DatabaseReference usersRef = FirebaseDatabase.getInstance().getReference("users");
String key = usersRef.push().getKey();
usersRef.child(key).setValue(new User("Charlie", 88));
```

> **Why `.push()`:** Generates a unique, chronologically-sortable key (e.g., `-NxYz123ABC`). This prevents key collisions when many clients write simultaneously.

### Step 6 — Handle Disconnect Cleanup

```java
ref.onDisconnect().removeValue();
```

> **Why:** When the user's app process dies, the server automatically deletes the node. This is essential for "online/offline" indicators in chat apps.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Pick Which?

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 board often asks a 7-mark question: *"Compare SQLite, Realm, and Firebase Realtime Database and state when you would use each."*

| Scenario | Best Choice | Justification |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Offline-first note-taking app | **SQLite** | Data is local; SQL queries are powerful for filtering |
| E-commerce catalog with 50K products | **Realm** | Object graph, blazing speed, no SQL overhead |
| Multi-user chat application | **Firebase RTDB** | Built-in real-time push, presence, offline queue |
| Banking transaction log (auditable) | **SQLite** | ACID compliance, SQL audit queries |
| Multiplayer game leaderboard | **Firebase RTDB** | WebSocket push, sub-100ms latency |
| Internal employee directory | **Realm** | Live updates, strong object relationships |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SQLite Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AppLayer["Application Layer Activity or Fragment"] --> Helper["SQLiteOpenHelper Subclass"]
    Helper --> OnCreate["onCreate Method CREATE TABLE SQL"]
    Helper --> OnUpgrade["onUpgrade Method ALTER or DROP"]
    Helper --> DBInstance["SQLiteDatabase Instance"]
    DBInstance --> CRUD["CRUD Methods insert update delete query"]
    CRUD --> File["students.db file on internal storage"]
    File --> Cursor["Cursor Object returned to UI"]
    Cursor --> UI["ListView or RecyclerView Display"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The application code never touches the `.db` file directly. It must always go through `SQLiteOpenHelper` → `SQLiteDatabase` → CRUD methods → file. The `Cursor` is the return bridge back to the UI layer.

## 4.2 Realm Object Mapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Class["Student extends RealmObject"] --> Reflect["Realm Reflection at Runtime"]
    Reflect --> File["student.realm File"]
    Transaction["realm.executeTransaction Block"] --> File
    Query["realm.where Student class RQL String"] --> File
    File --> LiveResults["Live RealmResults"]
    LiveResults --> Listener["addChangeListener Callback"]
    Listener --> UIUpdate["UI Auto Updates"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice there is **no SQL layer** and **no `Cursor`**. The class itself becomes the schema. Queries return **live** objects that notify the UI on any change.

## 4.3 Firebase Realtime Database Sync Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientA["Client A Android App"] --> WS1["WebSocket Persistent"]
    ClientB["Client B Android App"] --> WS2["WebSocket Persistent"]
    ClientC["Client C Web App"] --> WS3["WebSocket Persistent"]
    WS1 --> Cloud["Firebase Cloud Server JSON Tree"]
    WS2 --> Cloud
    WS3 --> Cloud
    Cloud --> Rules["Security Rules Validation"]
    Rules --> Cloud
    Cloud --> CacheA["Local Disk Cache Client A"]
    Cloud --> CacheB["Local Disk Cache Client B"]
    Cloud --> CacheC["Local Memory Cache Client C"]
    CacheA --> Offline["Offline Queue setPersistenceEnabled true"]
    Offline --> SyncBack["Auto Sync When Online"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The cloud server is the **single source of truth**. All clients maintain a persistent WebSocket. The local cache ensures offline functionality — writes are queued and synced later.

## 4.4 Three-Way Comparison Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need a Mobile Database?"] --> Q1{"Is data shared\nacross users\nin real time?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Firebase["Use Firebase Realtime Database"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is the app\nobject heavy\nwith relationships?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Realm["Use Realm"]
    Q2 -- No --> SQLite["Use SQLite"]
    Firebase --> Note1["Real-time push, WebSocket, presence"]
    Realm --> Note2["Object graph, live results, fast reads"]
    SQLite --> Note3["SQL queries, ACID, mature ecosystem"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **exam-ready decision tree** you can redraw in your answer sheet. Start with the *real-time* question — it is the strongest differentiator.

## 4.5 Data Flow Comparison Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SQLite_Layer["SQLite Pipeline"]
        S1["Java Code"] --> S2["ContentValues"] --> S3["SQL Statement"] --> S4["Cursor"]
    end
    subgraph Realm_Layer["Realm Pipeline"]
        R1["Java Code"] --> R2["RealmObject"] --> R3["Live RealmResults"]
    end
    subgraph Firebase_Layer["Firebase Pipeline"]
        F1["Java Code"] --> F2["DatabaseReference"] --> F3["DataSnapshot via Listener"]
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three parallel data flow paths. Notice how Realm eliminates the **mapping step** present in SQLite. Firebase eliminates the **manual fetch** step present in both local DBs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 PE Course Pattern):**
> - Part A: 3 marks each, short answer
> - Part B: 14 marks each, with internal choice (a) and (b) sub-parts
> - Total Module Weight: ~20% of Continuous Assessment + ESE

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q: Differentiate between SQLite and Firebase Realtime Database in terms of storage location, query language, and real-time capabilities. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (Board-Expected Wording):**

- **Storage location:** SQLite stores data as a local `.db` file inside the app's private folder. Firebase Realtime Database stores data on Google's cloud, with a local cache mirror on the device. (1 mark)
- **Query language:** SQLite uses standard **SQL** (Structured Query Language) with `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`. Firebase uses **path-based references** with helper methods like `orderByChild()` and `equalTo()`. (1 mark)
- **Real-time capabilities:** SQLite is purely local and has **no built-in real-time sync**. Firebase has **native real-time push** via persistent WebSockets, so changes propagate to all connected clients within milliseconds. (1 mark)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: What is a `RealmObject`? Why is Realm considered an "object-oriented" database? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A `RealmObject` is a Java/Kotlin class that extends the `RealmObject` base class, making its instances persistable by the Realm engine. (1 mark)

Realm is called object-oriented because:
1. The class itself **defines the schema** — no separate SQL `CREATE TABLE` is needed. (1 mark)
2. Data is stored as a **native object graph**, preserving references and relationships directly, eliminating the need for an ORM mapping layer. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option

### `[KTU University Exam - Model Question A]`

**Q (a):** Explain the architecture of SQLite. With a neat diagram, describe the `SQLiteOpenHelper` class and its lifecycle methods. Write a complete Android program to insert, retrieve, update, and delete student records (id, name, marks) using SQLite. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Board-Key Style):**

**Architecture (2 marks):**

SQLite is a serverless, zero-configuration, transactional SQL engine embedded into the Android OS. It is ACID-compliant and stores the entire database as a single `.db` file inside the app's private folder at `/data/data/<package_name>/databases/`.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    App["App Code"] --> H["SQLiteOpenHelper"]
    H --> OC["onCreate"]
    H --> OU["onUpgrade"]
    H --> DB["SQLiteDatabase"]
    DB --> F["student.db File"]
```

**`SQLiteOpenHelper` lifecycle (2 marks):**

- `onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db)`: invoked **once** when the database file is created for the first time. Used to execute `CREATE TABLE` statements.
- `onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldVersion, int newVersion)`: invoked when `DB_VERSION` is incremented. Used to handle schema migration via `ALTER TABLE` or `DROP TABLE`.

**Complete CRUD Code (3 marks):**

```java
public class DBHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper {
    public DBHelper(Context c) { super(c, "school.db", null, 1); }

    @Override public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) {
        db.execSQL("CREATE TABLE student(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, name TEXT, marks INTEGER)");
    }
    @Override public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int o, int n) {
        db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS student");
        onCreate(db);
    }

    public long insertStudent(String n, int m) {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getWritableDatabase();
        ContentValues cv = new ContentValues();
        cv.put("name", n); cv.put("marks", m);
        return db.insert("student", null, cv);
    }

    public Cursor getAllStudents() {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getReadableDatabase();
        return db.rawQuery("SELECT * FROM student", null);
    }

    public int updateMarks(int id, int newMarks) {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getWritableDatabase();
        ContentValues cv = new ContentValues();
        cv.put("marks", newMarks);
        return db.update("student", cv, "id=?", new String[]{String.valueOf(id)});
    }

    public int deleteStudent(int id) {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getWritableDatabase();
        return db.delete("student", "id=?", new String[]{String.valueOf(id)});
    }
}
```

---

**Q (b):** Explain the Firebase Realtime Database data model. With a code snippet, demonstrate how to write data, read data with a `ValueEventListener`, and enable offline persistence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Data Model (2 marks):**

Firebase RTDB stores data as a **single huge JSON tree**. Each node is a key-value pair. Values can be primitives (string, number, boolean) or nested objects. Unlike relational DBs, there are no tables or rows.

**Offline Persistence Setup (1 mark):**

```java
FirebaseDatabase.getInstance().setPersistenceEnabled(true);
```

> This call must be made **before any other Firebase DB reference is created**, typically inside the `onCreate()` of the `Application` subclass.

**Write Operation (2 marks):**

```java
DatabaseReference ref = FirebaseDatabase.getInstance().getReference("users");
ref.child("uid_alice").child("score").setValue(95);
```

**Read with Real-Time Listener (2 marks):**

```java
DatabaseReference ref = FirebaseDatabase.getInstance().getReference("users/uid_alice/score");
ref.addValueEventListener(new ValueEventListener() {
    @Override
    public void onDataChange(DataSnapshot snap) {
        Integer score = snap.getValue(Integer.class);
        textView.setText("Score: " + score);
    }
    @Override
    public void onCancelled(DatabaseError err) {
        Log.e("FB", err.getMessage());
    }
});
```

> The listener fires **on every change** to that node — pushed by Firebase's persistent WebSocket.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

### `[KTU University Exam - Model Question B]`

**Q (a):** Explain the Realm database model. Compare Realm with SQLite in terms of performance, schema, and query style. Write a Java program to insert and query a `Student` object using Realm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Realm Model (2 marks):**

Realm is a **mobile-first object database**. Classes extending `RealmObject` are auto-persisted. There is no SQL, no `Cursor`, and no `ContentValues`. Data is stored as a native object graph in a `.realm` file.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SQLite | Realm |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Performance | Adequate | 2–10× faster (native storage) |
| Schema | Fixed via `CREATE TABLE` | Class-based, dynamic |
| Query style | SQL strings | Realm Query Language (RQL) |
| Live updates | Manual `notifyDataSetChanged` | Built-in `addChangeListener` |

**Realm CRUD Code (2 marks):**

```java
Realm.init(context);
Realm realm = Realm.getDefaultInstance();

realm.executeTransaction(r -> {
    Student s = r.createObject(Student.class, 1);
    s.setName("Alice");
    s.setMarks(92);
});

RealmResults<Student> result = realm.where(Student.class)
                                     .greaterThan("marks", 80)
                                     .findAll();
```

---

**Q (b):** Write short notes on: (i) `SQLiteOpenHelper`, (ii) `RealmChangeListener`, (iii) `FirebaseDatabase.getReference()`, (iv) `ContentValues`, (v) `Cursor`, (vi) `addValueEventListener`, (vii) `RealmConfiguration`. **(7 marks — 1 mark each)**

**Model Answers (Concise):**

- **(i) `SQLiteOpenHelper`** → Abstract class that manages DB creation and version upgrades; provides `onCreate` and `onUpgrade` callbacks.
- **(ii) `RealmChangeListener`** → Listener attached to `RealmResults`; fires whenever the result set changes — enables live UI updates.
- **(iii) `FirebaseDatabase.getReference(path)`** → Returns a `DatabaseReference` pointing to a node in the JSON tree; the primary entry point for read/write.
- **(iv) `ContentValues`** → Key-value map used to pass column data to SQLite insert/update methods; safer than string concatenation.
- **(v) `Cursor`** → Interface representing a row-by-row result set from a SQLite query; must be `.close()`d to avoid memory leaks.
- **(vi) `addValueEventListener`** → Attaches a real-time listener to a Firebase reference; callback fires on every change pushed from the server.
- **(vii) `RealmConfiguration`** → Builder object that defines the Realm file name, schema version, and migration policy; passed to `Realm.getInstance()`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `c.close()` on Cursor** → 1 mark deduction. Always show cursor close in a `finally` block.
> 2. **Confusing Realm with Room** → Room is an *ORM over SQLite*; Realm is a *native object DB*. Examiners WILL deduct 2 marks for mixing them up.
> 3. **Writing raw SQL strings with user input** → Security violation. Always use parameterized queries (`?` placeholders + `String[]`).
> 4. **Forgetting `setPersistenceEnabled(true)` placement** → Must be called **before** any `getReference()` call.
> 5. **Not mentioning transactions for Realm writes** → Realm writes outside `executeTransaction` will throw `IllegalStateException`. Examiners check this.
> 6. **Using `addListenerForSingleValueEvent` where live updates are required** → Mismatch between listener type and use case. Pick the right listener for the question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Pin This):**

- **SQLite** is a serverless, file-based SQL engine; default Android DB; uses `SQLiteOpenHelper`.
- **Realm** is an object-oriented DB; classes extend `RealmObject`; no SQL, no Cursor; uses `executeTransaction`.
- **Firebase RTDB** is a cloud-hosted NoSQL JSON tree; supports real-time push via WebSocket; requires `setPersistenceEnabled(true)` for offline.
- **Cursor** = row pointer in SQLite; must be closed manually.
- **ContentValues** = key-value wrapper for SQLite inserts/updates.
- **`addValueEventListener`** = live Firebase listener; fires on every server push.
- **`addListenerForSingleValueEvent`** = one-time Firebase read; use for non-live data.
- **RealmResults are LIVE** — auto-update on underlying data change.
- **Firebase rules** govern who can read/write which paths; always validate `auth.uid` for security.
- **Decision rule:** Need real-time multi-user sync → Firebase. Need object-heavy local data → Realm. Need classic relational queries → SQLite.
- **Exam keywords to use:** "ACID-compliant", "WebSocket", "JSON tree", "object graph", "transactional", "schemaless", "offline-first".

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 3 Notes — Working with Databases**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Storage and Management in Mobile Apps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT744 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Storage and Management in Mobile Apps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📚 Module 3: Data Storage and Management in Mobile Apps

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Data Storage and Management in Mobile Apps** refers to the systematic process of persisting, retrieving, organizing, securing, and synchronizing data within a mobile application across volatile (RAM) and non-volatile (disk) memory layers. It encompasses structured storage (SQLite, Room), unstructured storage (files, key-value pairs), and distributed storage (cloud, network APIs) — collectively forming the **Persistence Layer** of the MVC/MVVM architecture in modern mobile frameworks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Filing Cabinet of a Mobile App" 🗄️

Imagine your mobile app is a **small office**:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Mobile App Equivalent |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Office Notice Board** | Sticky notes for quick reminders | `SharedPreferences` (key-value) |
| **Personal Drawer** | Your private documents, locked | **Internal Storage** (app-private) |
| **Shared Conference Room Cabinet** | Documents anyone in the office can use | **External Storage** (public) |
| **Indexed Filing System** | Sorted, searchable records | `SQLite Database` / Room |
| **Cloud Archive Service** | Backup in a remote warehouse | **Remote Server / Firebase / REST APIs** |
| **Inbox/Outbox Tray** | Messages flowing in & out | **Content Providers** |

Just as a well-organized office retrieves files quickly, an app must store and retrieve data **efficiently, securely, and reliably**, even after the app is closed, the device is rebooted, or the user is offline.

### 1.3 Why Data Storage Matters in Mobile Apps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — CO3 Mapping):**
> Mobile applications must handle data persistence because:
> - The **OS may kill apps** anytime (low-memory situations).
> - **User experience** demands settings, login tokens, and progress to be retained.
> - **Offline-first design** is now an industry standard.
> - Data must survive **device reboots, app updates, and crashes**.

The **standard metrics** emphasized by the KTU syllabus for evaluating a storage mechanism are:

- **Persistence** — Does the data survive app termination?
- **Confidentiality** — Can other apps access the data?
- **Performance** — Read/Write speed in milliseconds (**ms**).
- **Capacity** — Storage size limits (typically **MB to GB**).
- **Concurrency** — Can multiple threads access it safely?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Data Storage Mechanisms

Mobile app data storage is broadly classified into **three categories** based on **storage location** and **data structure**.

### 🅰️ Local (On-Device) Storage

#### 2.1.1 SharedPreferences (Key-Value Store)

`SharedPreferences` is Android's lightweight, XML-based key-value persistence mechanism. It is best suited for storing **small primitive data** such as user settings, login flags, theme preferences, and tokens.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Acquire** an instance of `SharedPreferences` using `getSharedPreferences(name, mode)`.
2. **Edit** the data via `SharedPreferences.Editor`.
3. **Apply / Commit** the changes:
   - `apply()` → Asynchronous, faster (writes to memory instantly, to disk later).
   - `commit()` → Synchronous, slower, returns success boolean.
4. **Retrieve** data using getter methods (`getString`, `getInt`, `getBoolean`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Types Supported:** `boolean`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `String`, `StringSet`.
> **Storage Location:** `/data/data/<package_name>/shared_prefs/<name>.xml`
> **Visibility:** App-private (other apps cannot read it).

#### 2.1.2 Internal Storage

Internal storage refers to the **private file system** allocated exclusively to your app on the device's internal memory.

- **Path:** `/data/data/<package_name>/files/`
- **Access:** Only your app (and root user) can read/write.
- **Best for:** Sensitive files, user-generated content, app databases.
- **Lifecycle:** Files are **removed when the app is uninstalled**.

**Code Snippet (Conceptual — Java/Kotlin):**
```java
FileOutputStream fos = openFileOutput("userdata.txt", Context.MODE_PRIVATE);
fos.write(data.getBytes());
fos.close();
```

#### 2.1.3 External Storage

External storage refers to the **shared storage area** (SD card or emulated internal storage), accessible to other apps with proper permissions.

- **Path:** `/storage/emulated/0/...` (public directories like `Documents`, `Pictures`).
- **Permissions (Android 10+):** Scoped Storage restricts direct access.
- **Best for:** Large media files, files that need to survive app uninstall.
- **Risk:** Other apps can read/modify — **not secure** without encryption.

#### 2.1.4 SQLite Database

SQLite is a **relational database engine** embedded in Android. It is **serverless, zero-configuration, transactional, and ACID-compliant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACID Properties (Examiner Favourite):**
> - **A**tomicity — All or nothing transactions.
> - **C**onsistency — Valid state transitions only.
> - **I**solation — Concurrent transactions don't interfere.
> - **D**urability — Committed data persists across crashes.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Lightweight (≈ **600 KB** library footprint).
- Supports **SQL standards** (CREATE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT, JOIN).
- Data is stored in a single `.db` file in internal storage.
- Managed via `SQLiteOpenHelper` class (raw approach) or **Room** (modern approach).

#### 2.1.5 Room Persistence Library

Room is a **persistence library** (part of Android Jetpack / Architecture Components) that provides an abstraction layer over SQLite.

**Three Core Components:**

| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `@Entity` | Defines a database table as a Java/Kotlin class |
| `@Dao` | Data Access Object — defines database operations |
| `@Database` | Marks the database class; holds DAOs and version info |

**Advantages over Raw SQLite:**
- **Compile-time SQL verification** (catches errors at build time).
- **Reduced boilerplate** code.
- **Built-in support for LiveData, Flow, and Coroutines**.
- **Easy migration** with `Migration` objects.

#### 2.1.6 File Storage (Cache & Raw Files)

- **Cache Storage:** `/data/data/<package_name>/cache/` — temporary files; OS may clear it.
- **Raw Files:** Arbitrary binary or text files (PDFs, images, JSON).

### 🅱️ Network (Remote) Storage

#### 2.1.7 REST APIs & Cloud Services

Data is stored on a **remote server** and accessed via HTTP/HTTPS using REST principles.

**Common Data Exchange Formats:**
- **JSON** (JavaScript Object Notation) — most common, lightweight.
- **XML** (eXtensible Markup Language) — older, heavier, SOAP-based.

**Popular Cloud BaaS (Backend-as-a-Service) Providers:**
- **Firebase Realtime Database / Firestore** (Google)
- **AWS Amplify / DynamoDB** (Amazon)
- **Azure Mobile Apps** (Microsoft)
- **Supabase, Parse**

#### 2.1.8 Content Providers

Content Providers enable **inter-app data sharing** in a secure, controlled manner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples of System Content Providers:**
> - `ContactsContract` — Phone contacts
> - `MediaStore` — Images, audio, video
> - `CallLog` — Call history

**URI Pattern:** `content://authority/path/id`

### 🅲️ Hybrid Approaches

#### 2.1.9 Caching & Offline Synchronization

Modern apps use a **hybrid model**:
- Frequently accessed data → **cached locally** (Room, SharedPreferences).
- Master data → **fetched from server** (REST API).
- **Sync mechanisms** (WorkManager, SyncAdapter) reconcile local & remote data.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Definition / Formula | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **SharedPreferences** | Key-value XML store for primitives | Settings, flags, tokens |
| 2 | **Internal Storage** | App-private file system at `/data/data/<pkg>/files/` | Sensitive files, DBs |
| 3 | **External Storage** | Public/shared storage with permission | Large media files |
| 4 | **SQLite** | Embedded relational DB; ACID-compliant | Structured relational data |
| 5 | **Room** | ORM abstraction over SQLite with annotation processing | Modern Android persistence |
| 6 | **ContentProvider** | URI-based inter-process data sharing | Accessing system/user data |
| 7 | **REST/JSON** | HTTP-based remote data exchange over network | Cloud-backed apps |
| 8 | **Cache Directory** | `/cache/` — OS-clearable temporary storage | Images, transient data |
| 9 | **WorkManager** | API for deferrable, guaranteed background tasks | Sync, periodic uploads |
| 10 | **Scoped Storage** | Android 10+ sandboxed external storage model | Modern file access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (Exam Tip):**
> - **Small primitives** → SharedPreferences
> - **Relational/structured data** → Room / SQLite
> - **Large media** → Internal/External file storage
> - **Cross-app sharing** → ContentProvider
> - **Remote/cloud data** → REST API + local cache

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | Cart data in Room; product catalog from REST API; user session in SharedPreferences |
| **Banking Apps** | Encrypted SharedPreferences for tokens; SQLite for transaction history |
| **Social Media (Instagram)** | ContentProvider for media; Room for drafts; REST for feeds |
| **Health & Fitness** | SQLite for offline step logs; Firebase for cross-device sync |
| **Gaming** | Local SQLite for high scores; cloud save via REST API |
| **IoT Control Apps** | SharedPreferences for last-known device state; WorkManager for sync |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Storage

The KTU 2024 syllabus frequently tests the **"Which storage to use when?"** question. The following decision flow resolves the choice.

### Step 1 — Identify Data Type

$$
\text{Data Type} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Primitive (bool, int, String)} & \Rightarrow \text{SharedPreferences} \\
\text{Relational (tables, joins)} & \Rightarrow \text{Room / SQLite} \\
\text{Binary blob (image, video)} & \Rightarrow \text{File Storage} \\
\text{Shared with other apps} & \Rightarrow \text{ContentProvider} \\
\text{Stored on cloud} & \Rightarrow \text{REST API + Local Cache}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Constraints

$$
\text{Constraint Vector} = \{\text{Size}, \text{Security}, \text{Speed}, \text{Concurrency}\}
$$

- If **Size $\le$ 1 MB** and **data is primitive** → SharedPreferences wins.
- If **Size $\ge$ 1 MB** or **structured** → Room/SQLite.
- If **need inter-app access** → ContentProvider.

### Step 3 — Performance Trade-off Table

| Storage | Read Speed | Write Speed | Max Practical Size | Security |
|---------|-----------|------------|-------------------|----------|
| SharedPreferences | **Very Fast** (~1 ms) | Fast (apply) | **< 1 MB** | Medium (app-private) |
| Room/SQLite | Fast (~5-10 ms per query) | Medium | **GBs** | High (app-private + encryption) |
| Internal File | Fast (I/O bound) | Fast | **App quota** | High (app-private) |
| External File | Medium (depends on SD) | Medium | **GBs** | Low (public) |
| REST API | **Slow** (network) | Slow | **Unlimited** | TLS-encrypted |
| ContentProvider | Medium | Medium | Varies | Permission-controlled |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Room Database CRUD Operation

Below is a **complete, step-by-step implementation** of a Room database with full CRUD operations.

### Step 1 — Add Dependencies (`build.gradle`)

```gradle
implementation "androidx.room:room-runtime:2.6.1"
annotationProcessor "androidx.room:room-compiler:2.6.1"
```

### Step 2 — Define the Entity (Table)

```java
@Entity(tableName = "students")
public class Student {
    @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true)
    private int id;

    @ColumnInfo(name = "student_name")
    private String name;

    @ColumnInfo(name = "student_age")
    private int age;

    // Constructors, getters, setters
    public Student(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
    }
}
```

### Step 3 — Define the DAO (Operations)

```java
@Dao
public interface StudentDao {

    @Insert
    void insert(Student student);

    @Update
    void update(Student student);

    @Delete
    void delete(Student student);

    @Query("SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY student_name ASC")
    LiveData<List<Student>> getAllStudents();

    @Query("SELECT * FROM students WHERE id = :studentId")
    Student getStudentById(int studentId);
}
```

### Step 4 — Define the Database Class

```java
@Database(entities = {Student.class}, version = 1)
public abstract class AppDatabase extends RoomDatabase {
    public abstract StudentDao studentDao();

    private static AppDatabase INSTANCE;

    public static synchronized AppDatabase getInstance(Context context) {
        if (INSTANCE == null) {
            INSTANCE = Room.databaseBuilder(
                context.getApplicationContext(),
                AppDatabase.class,
                "student_db"
            ).build();
        }
        return INSTANCE;
    }
}
```

### Step 5 — Use in Activity / ViewModel

```java
AppDatabase db = AppDatabase.getInstance(this);
StudentDao dao = db.studentDao();

// Create
dao.insert(new Student("Ananya", 21));

// Read (LiveData observation)
dao.getAllStudents().observe(this, students -> {
    Log.d("DB", "Students count: " + students.size());
});
```

### Step 6 — Verify SQL Generated by Room

The Room compiler translates the above Java annotations into raw SQL:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TABLE students (} \\
\text{id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,} \\
\text{student\_name TEXT,} \\
\text{student\_age INTEGER} \\
\text{);}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{INSERT INTO students (student\_name, student\_age) VALUES (?, ?)} \\
\text{SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY student\_name ASC} \\
\text{UPDATE students SET student\_name = ?, student\_age = ? WHERE id = ?} \\
\text{DELETE FROM students WHERE id = ?}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: SharedPreferences Lifecycle

The complete state transition of a SharedPreferences read/write cycle is as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_0 &: \text{App starts} \rightarrow \text{SharedPreferences loaded into RAM} \\
\text{State}_1 &: \text{Editor.putString("key", "value")} \rightarrow \text{In-memory change} \\
\text{State}_2 &: \text{Editor.apply()} \rightarrow \text{Async write to disk} \\
\text{State}_3 &: \text{Editor.commit()} \rightarrow \text{Sync write, returns boolean} \\
\text{State}_4 &: \text{getString("key", default)} \rightarrow \text{Returns value or default}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pseudocode Logic:**

```java
SharedPreferences prefs = getSharedPreferences("MyPrefs", MODE_PRIVATE);
SharedPreferences.Editor editor = prefs.edit();

editor.putBoolean("is_logged_in", true);    // Write
editor.putString("username", "Karthik");     // Write
editor.apply();                              // Asynchronous commit

boolean loggedIn = prefs.getBoolean("is_logged_in", false);
String user = prefs.getString("username", "Guest");
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `apply()` over `commit()`?**
> - `apply()` writes changes to memory **immediately** and to disk **asynchronously**, ensuring UI responsiveness.
> - `commit()` blocks the calling thread until disk write completes — use only when you need confirmation of success.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: JSON Parsing via REST API

When a mobile app fetches data from a remote server, the standard flow is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: HTTP Request} \rightarrow \text{GET https://api.example.com/students} \\
\text{Step 2: Server Response} \rightarrow \text{JSON string in body} \\
\text{Step 3: Parse JSON} \rightarrow \text{Convert to Java/Kotlin objects} \\
\text{Step 4: Persist Locally} \rightarrow \text{Save in Room for offline access} \\
\text{Step 5: Update UI} \rightarrow \text{LiveData / Flow notifies observers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample JSON Response:**

```json
{
  "status": "success",
  "data": [
    {"id": 1, "name": "Ananya", "age": 21},
    {"id": 2, "name": "Rahul", "age": 22}
  ]
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Libraries Used in Practice:**
> - **Retrofit** — Type-safe HTTP client.
> - **Gson / Moshi / kotlinx.serialization** — JSON parsing.
> - **OkHttp** — Underlying HTTP engine.
> - **Glide / Coil / Picasso** — Image loading from network.

---

## 3.5 SQLite vs Room — Comparative Derivation

| Parameter | Raw SQLite (`SQLiteOpenHelper`) | Room Persistence Library |
|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **SQL Verification** | Runtime (crashes on bad SQL) | **Compile-time** (no runtime crashes) |
| **Boilerplate Code** | High (Cursor parsing, manual mapping) | **Low** (annotations handle it) |
| **Reactive Support** | Manual (ContentObserver) | **Built-in** (LiveData, Flow) |
| **Migration Support** | Manual (version-based) | **Automated** (`Migration` class) |
| **Testing** | Difficult | **Easy** (in-memory DB) |
| **Learning Curve** | Steeper | **Gentler** for Java/Kotlin devs |
| **Performance** | Slightly faster (no annotation processing) | Negligible overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verdict:** Room is the **recommended** approach for new Android apps (per Google's official guidance). Raw SQLite is now mostly used in legacy codebases or low-level system apps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mobile App Data Storage Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UI[UI Layer Activity Fragment View] --> VM[ViewModel MVVM Pattern]

    VM --> REPO[Repository Layer Single Source of Truth]

    REPO --> LOCAL[Local Data Sources]
    REPO --> REMOTE[Remote Data Sources]

    LOCAL --> SP[SharedPreferences XML File Key Value]
    LOCAL --> ROOM[Room Database SQLite Wrapper]
    LOCAL --> INT[Internal File Storage App Private]
    LOCAL --> CACHE[Cache Directory Temporary Files]
    LOCAL --> CP[ContentProvider URI Based Access]

    REMOTE --> REST[REST API HTTPS JSON Response]
    REMOTE --> FB[Firebase Firestore Realtime DB]
    REMOTE --> AWS[AWS Amplify Cloud Storage]

    ROOM --> SQLITE[SQLite Engine ACID Compliant]
    SQLITE --> DBFILE[DB File data data pkg databases]

    CP --> SYSDATA[System Data Contacts Media CallLog]
    CP --> APPDATA[Other App Data With Permission]

    REST --> NET[Network Layer OkHttp Retrofit Volley]
    NET --> SERVER[Remote Server Cloud Database]

    VM -.observes.-> LIVEDATA[LiveData Flow RxJava Reactive Stream]
    LIVEDATA --> UI
```

## 4.2 Storage Selection Flowchart

```mermaid

    START([Need to Store Data]) --> Q1{What type of data?}

    Q1 -->|Primitive small| Q2{Need to share with other apps?}
    Q1 -->|Structured relational| ROOM[Use Room SQLite]
    Q1 -->|Large binary files| Q3{Sensitive or public?}

    Q2 -->|No| SP[Use SharedPreferences]
    Q2 -->|Yes| CP1[Use ContentProvider]

    Q3 -->|Sensitive private| INT[Use Internal Storage]
    Q3 -->|Public large media| EXT[Use External Storage with permission]

    ROOM --> DONE([Data Persisted Successfully])
    SP --> DONE
    CP1 --> DONE
    INT --> DONE
    EXT --> DONE

    Q1 -->|Remote cloud data| REST[Use REST API with local cache Room]
    REST --> DONE
```

## 4.3 Room Database Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Application Code] --> DAO[DAO Interface Annotated Methods]
    DAO --> ROOM[Room Compiler Annotation Processor]
    ROOM --> IMPL[Generated DAO Implementation]
    IMPL --> SQL[Raw SQL Queries]
    SQL --> SQLITE[SQLite Database Engine]
    SQLITE --> DBFILE[DB File On Disk]

    ENT[Entity Classes] --> ROOM
    DB[Database Class Abstract] --> ROOM

    OBS[LiveData Flow Observers] --> IMPL
    IMPL -.notifies.-> OBS

    style APP fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B
    style DAO fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17

    style SQLITE fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40
    style DBFILE fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#263238
```

## 4.4 Data Synchronization Flow (Offline-First Architecture)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant UI as App UI
    participant DB as Local Room DB
    participant WM as WorkManager
    participant API as Remote REST API
    participant SVR as Cloud Server

    U->>UI: Performs Action Create Read Update Delete
    UI->>DB: Write/Read from local DB immediately
    DB-->>UI: Returns data synchronously

    Note over DB,WM: Periodic background sync
    WM->>DB: Check for unsynced records
    DB-->>WM: Returns pending changes
    WM->>API: Send HTTP POST PUT with delta
    API->>SVR: Forward request to server
    SVR-->>API: HTTP 200 OK with response
    API-->>WM: Success response
    WM->>DB: Mark records as synced

    DB-->>UI: LiveData notifies observers
    UI-->>U: Updated UI display
```
