# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define SharedPreferences in Android. List any four data types that can be stored in it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> `SharedPreferences` is an Android API that allows apps to store and retrieve small amounts of primitive data as **key-value pairs** in an XML file located in the app's private internal storage.

> **Data Types (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. `boolean` — for true/false flags (e.g., isLoggedIn)
> 2. `int` — for integer counters or IDs
> 3. `float` — for decimal values (e.g., volume level)
> 4. `String` — for text values (e.g., username)
> 5. `long` — for timestamps
> 6. `StringSet` — for a collection of strings

> **Storage Path:** `/data/data/<package_name>/shared_prefs/<file_name>.xml`

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is a ContentProvider? Give two examples of system-defined ContentProviders in Android.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> A **ContentProvider** is a component in Android that manages access to a structured set of data, enabling data sharing across different applications in a secure, URI-based manner.

> **Two Examples (2 Marks — 1 each):**
> 1. **`ContactsContract`** — Used to access the device's contact list. Example URI: `content://com.android.contacts/contacts`
> 2. **`MediaStore`** — Used to access media files (images, audio, video) on the device. Example URI: `content://media/external/images/media`

> **URI Format:** `content://<authority>/<table>/<id>`

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Explain the architecture and components of the Room Persistence Library. How does it differ from raw SQLite? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Room is a **persistence library** provided as part of Android Jetpack (Architecture Components) that provides an abstraction layer over SQLite. It was introduced to make database interactions more robust, type-safe, and less boilerplate-heavy.

**Three Core Components (3 Marks):**

1. **@Entity** — A Java/Kotlin class annotated with `@Entity` represents a **table** in the database. Each field in the class corresponds to a **column**. The `@PrimaryKey` annotation marks the primary key.

   ```java
   @Entity(tableName = "employees")
   public class Employee {
       @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true)
       public int id;
       public String name;
       public double salary;
   }
   ```

2. **@Dao (Data Access Object)** — An interface annotated with `@Dao` contains methods defining **CRUD operations**. Room automatically generates the implementation at compile time.

   ```java
   @Dao
   public interface EmployeeDao {
       @Insert
       void insert(Employee employee);
       @Query("SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > :minSalary")
       List<Employee> getHighEarners(double minSalary);
   }
   ```

3. **@Database** — An abstract class annotated with `@Database` serves as the **main access point**. It lists all entities, the database version, and is typically a singleton.

   ```java
   @Database(entities = {Employee.class}, version = 1)
   public abstract class AppDatabase extends RoomDatabase {
       public abstract EmployeeDao employeeDao();
   }
   ```

**Differences from Raw SQLite (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Raw SQLite | Room |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| **SQL Verification** | Runtime (crash on error) | Compile-time (no crash) |
| **Code Volume** | High boilerplate | Concise annotations |
| **Reactive Support** | Manual | Built-in LiveData/Flow |
| **Testing** | Difficult | Easy with in-memory DB |
| **Migration** | Manual | Automated via `Migration` |

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
Room is the **modern recommended approach** for SQLite operations in Android, offering better safety, less code, and architectural alignment with MVVM.

---

#### **(b) Design and implement a Room database for a "Student Attendance Tracker" app that stores student ID, name, date, and attendance status (Present/Absent). Include Entity, DAO, and Database class. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Entity Class (2 Marks):**

```java
@Entity(tableName = "attendance")
public class Attendance {
    @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true)
    private int id;

    @ColumnInfo(name = "student_id")
    private String studentId;

    @ColumnInfo(name = "student_name")
    private String studentName;

    @ColumnInfo(name = "attendance_date")
    private String date; // Format: yyyy-MM-dd

    @ColumnInfo(name = "status")
    private String status; // "Present" or "Absent"

    // Constructor
    public Attendance(String studentId, String studentName, String date, String status) {
        this.studentId = studentId;
        this.studentName = studentName;
        this.date = date;
        this.status = status;
    }

    // Getters and setters omitted for brevity
}
```

**DAO Interface (2 Marks):**

```java
@Dao
public interface AttendanceDao {

    @Insert
    void insertAttendance(Attendance attendance);

    @Update
    void updateAttendance(Attendance attendance);

    @Delete
    void deleteAttendance(Attendance attendance);

    @Query("SELECT * FROM attendance ORDER BY attendance_date DESC")
    LiveData<List<Attendance>> getAllAttendance();

    @Query("SELECT * FROM attendance WHERE attendance_date = :date")
    List<Attendance> getAttendanceByDate(String date);

    @Query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM attendance WHERE student_id = :sid AND status = 'Present'")
    int getPresentCount(String sid);
}
```

**Database Class (2 Marks):**

```java
@Database(entities = {Attendance.class}, version = 1, exportSchema = false)
public abstract class AppDatabase extends RoomDatabase {

    private static volatile AppDatabase INSTANCE;

    public abstract AttendanceDao attendanceDao();

    public static AppDatabase getDatabase(final Context context) {
        if (INSTANCE == null) {
            synchronized (AppDatabase.class) {
                if (INSTANCE == null) {
                    INSTANCE = Room.databaseBuilder(
                        context.getApplicationContext(),
                        AppDatabase.class,
                        "attendance_db"
                    ).build();
                }
            }
        }
        return INSTANCE;
    }
}
```

**Usage in Activity (1 Mark):**

```java
AppDatabase db = AppDatabase.getDatabase(this);
AttendanceDao dao = db.attendanceDao();

// Insert
dao.insertAttendance(new Attendance("S101", "Ananya", "2024-12-15", "Present"));

// Observe
dao.getAllAttendance().observe(this, list -> {
    Log.d("ATTENDANCE", "Total records: " + list.size());
});
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain different data storage options available in Android with suitable use-cases. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Android provides **six primary data storage mechanisms**, each suited to specific scenarios.

**1. SharedPreferences (1 Mark):**
- **What:** Key-value XML-based store for small primitive data.
- **Use Case:** Saving user settings (theme, language), login flags, authentication tokens.
- **Limit:** Not suitable for large or complex data.

**2. Internal Storage (1 Mark):**
- **What:** Private file system area allocated to the app at `/data/data/<pkg>/files/`.
- **Use Case:** Storing sensitive files like user-generated documents, profile pictures, JSON caches.
- **Feature:** Removed automatically when app is uninstalled.

**3. External Storage (1 Mark):**
- **What:** Public/shared storage on SD card or emulated internal storage.
- **Use Case:** Storing large media files (videos, high-resolution images) that need to persist after app uninstall.
- **Risk:** Requires runtime permissions; data is accessible to other apps (Android 10+ uses Scoped Storage).

**4. SQLite Database (1 Mark):**
- **What:** Embedded relational database engine supporting full SQL.
- **Use Case:** Storing structured, relational data such as product catalogs, transaction histories, contact lists.
- **Feature:** ACID-compliant, transactional, supports complex queries with JOINs.

**5. Room Persistence Library (1 Mark):**
- **What:** ORM-style abstraction over SQLite using annotations.
- **Use Case:** Modern Android apps needing a robust, type-safe persistence layer integrated with LiveData/Flow.
- **Advantage:** Compile-time SQL verification, less boilerplate.

**6. Network/Cloud Storage (1 Mark):**
- **What:** Data stored on remote servers, accessed via REST APIs or BaaS providers like Firebase.
- **Use Case:** Apps requiring multi-device sync, social media feeds, collaborative tools.
- **Feature:** Enables offline-first architecture when combined with local caching.

**7. Content Providers (1 Mark):**
- **What:** URI-based abstraction for inter-process data sharing.
- **Use Case:** Accessing system data (contacts, media, call logs) or exposing your app's data to other apps.

**Summary Table (if needed):**

| Storage | Data Type | Visibility | Best For |
|---------|-----------|------------|----------|
| SharedPreferences | Primitives | Private | Settings |
| Internal Storage | Files | Private | Sensitive files |
| External Storage | Files | Public (with permission) | Media |
| SQLite/Room | Relational | Private | Structured data |
| Network | Any | Server-side | Cloud sync |
| ContentProvider | Any | Permission-controlled | Cross-app sharing |

---

#### **(b) Write a program to demonstrate CRUD operations using SharedPreferences in an Android application. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Layout XML (`activity_main.xml`) (1 Mark)**

```xml
<LinearLayout xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:padding="20dp">

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/etUsername"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter username" />

    <EditText
        android:id="@+id/etEmail"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:hint="Enter email"
        android:inputType="textEmailAddress" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnSave"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Save" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnLoad"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Load" />

    <Button
        android:id="@+id/btnClear"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:text="Clear All" />

    <TextView
        android:id="@+id/tvResult"
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:textSize="18sp"
        android:padding="20dp" />
</LinearLayout>
```

**Step 2: MainActivity Java Code (6 Marks):**

```java
public class MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity {

    EditText etUsername, etEmail;
    Button btnSave, btnLoad, btnClear;
    TextView tvResult;
    SharedPreferences sharedPreferences;
    SharedPreferences.Editor editor;

    @Override
    protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
        setContentView(R.layout.activity_main);

        etUsername = findViewById(R.id.etUsername);
        etEmail = findViewById(R.id.etEmail);
        btnSave = findViewById(R.id.btnSave);
        btnLoad = findViewById(R.id.btnLoad);
        btnClear = findViewById(R.id.btnClear);
        tvResult = findViewById(R.id.tvResult);

        // Initialize SharedPreferences
        sharedPreferences = getSharedPreferences("UserData", MODE_PRIVATE);
        editor = sharedPreferences.edit();

        // CREATE / UPDATE
        btnSave.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            String username = etUsername.getText().toString();
            String email = etEmail.getText().toString();
            editor.putString("username", username);
            editor.putString("email", email);
            editor.putBoolean("isRegistered", true);
            editor.apply(); // Asynchronous write
            Toast.makeText(this, "Data Saved Successfully", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
        });

        // READ
        btnLoad.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            String username = sharedPreferences.getString("username", "Not Set");
            String email = sharedPreferences.getString("email", "Not Set");
            boolean isRegistered = sharedPreferences.getBoolean("isRegistered", false);
            tvResult.setText("Username: " + username +
                             "\nEmail: " + email +
                             "\nRegistered: " + isRegistered);
        });

        // DELETE
        btnClear.setOnClickListener(v -> {
            editor.clear();
            editor.apply();
            tvResult.setText("All Data Cleared");
            Toast.makeText(this, "Data Cleared", Toast.LENGTH_SHORT).show();
        });
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Methods (integrated above):**

- `getSharedPreferences("UserData", MODE_PRIVATE)` — Creates/opens an XML file named `UserData.xml`.
- `editor.putString(key, value)` — Writes a key-value pair.
- `editor.apply()` — Asynchronously commits the changes.
- `sharedPreferences.getString(key, defaultValue)` — Retrieves value; returns default if not found.
- `editor.clear()` — Removes all entries from the SharedPreferences file.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `apply()` with `commit()`** — `apply()` is asynchronous (fast, no return value); `commit()` is synchronous (slow, returns boolean). Students often mark them as identical — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting `MODE_PRIVATE`** — This is mandatory for app privacy. Other modes (`MODE_WORLD_READABLE`, `MODE_WORLD_WRITABLE`) are deprecated since API 17.
> 3. **Mixing up Room's `@Insert` vs `@Query`** — `@Insert`, `@Update`, `@Delete` are predefined annotations; `@Query` is for custom SQL. Students often write `@Query("INSERT ...")` which is **technically valid but not idiomatic** — deduct 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Missing `synchronized` block in Room singleton** — Room database must be a **singleton** to prevent memory leaks. Missing this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to add Room dependencies** in `build.gradle` — Without the compiler annotation processor, the app won't compile.
> 6. **Confusing Internal vs External Storage paths** — Remember: Internal is **app-private**, External requires **runtime permission**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ✅ **SharedPreferences** = lightweight key-value XML storage for **primitives only** (bool, int, float, long, String, StringSet).
- ✅ **Internal Storage** = app-private files at `/data/data/<pkg>/files/`; **secure** but deleted on uninstall.
- ✅ **External Storage** = public storage; needs **runtime permission** (Android 6+); uses **Scoped Storage** (Android 10+).
- ✅ **SQLite** = embedded relational DB, **ACID-compliant**, serverless, zero-config, ≈ **600 KB** footprint.
- ✅ **Room** = Jetpack ORM over SQLite with **`@Entity`, `@Dao`, `@Database`** annotations; provides **compile-time SQL verification**.
- ✅ **ContentProvider** = URI-based inter-app data sharing (`content://authority/table/id`).
- ✅ **REST/JSON** = remote data via **HTTP GET/POST/PUT/DELETE**; libraries: **Retrofit + Gson + OkHttp**.
- ✅ **Firebase** = BaaS with **Realtime DB** and **Firestore** for cloud sync.
- ✅ **WorkManager** = for **deferrable, guaranteed** background tasks like periodic sync.
- ✅ **Cache directory** = `/cache/`; OS may **auto-clear** it under storage pressure.
- ✅ **Always use `apply()`** over `commit()` in SharedPreferences for performance.
- ✅ **Always use singleton pattern** for Room database instance to prevent leaks.
- ✅ **MVVM Architecture** = UI ↔ ViewModel ↔ Repository ↔ (Local DB + Remote API).

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Yield Exam Pointers:**
> - "Differentiate SharedPreferences vs SQLite" → **Most asked question**.
> - "Explain Room architecture with components" → **14-mark favorite**.
> - "List storage options with use-cases" → **Frequently in Part A**.
> - **ACID properties of SQLite** → **Sure-shot 3-mark question**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Push Notifications and In-App Messaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT744 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing Push Notifications and In-App Messaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — IMPLEMENTING PUSH NOTIFICATIONS AND IN-APP MESSAGING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Push Notification** is a short, server-initiated message delivered to a mobile device through platform-specific infrastructure such as **Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM)** for Android or **Apple Push Notification service (APNs)** for iOS, displayed outside the application's active UI context (notification tray, lock screen, banner).
>
> **In-App Messaging** is a context-bound message rendered *inside* the live UI of an active application, typically driven by local triggers, in-session events, or real-time SDK listeners (e.g., Firebase In-App Messaging SDK), and disappears once the session ends.

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Menu Board

Imagine you walk into your favourite restaurant (**the app**). While eating, the manager brings a small printed card directly to your table listing today's special offer. **That is In-App Messaging** — relevant, contextual, only while you are inside.

Now imagine you are at home, the restaurant is closed, but your phone buzzes with a notification: *"🎉 50% off tonight only!"*. **That is a Push Notification** — it reaches you anywhere, anytime, even when the app is closed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT744 Module 3):**
> - Distinguish between **Push** (server-pushed, OS-managed display) and **In-App** (client-driven, UI-rendered).
> - Implement using **FCM** (cross-platform de-facto standard).
> - Handle **Notification Channels** (mandatory from Android 8.0 / API 26+).
> - Manage **Foreground, Background, and Terminated** lifecycle states.
> - Trigger **Local Notifications** without any backend.

### 📊 Key Metrics & Constants

| Term | Definition | Value / Standard |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **FCM Message Payload Limit** | Max size of notification data payload | **4096 bytes (4 KB)** |
| **Notification Title Limit** | Recommended display length | **~65 characters** |
| **APNs Token Refresh** | Token validity cycle | **~1 hour** (device-dependent) |
| **TTL (Time-To-Live)** | Validity of undelivered push | **0 – 2,419,200 sec (28 days)** |
| **Android Notification Channels** | Mandatory from | **API Level 26 (Android 8.0 Oreo)** |
| **In-App Message Display Frequency** | Default rule | **Once per device per campaign** |

---

## 2. Why Push Notifications Matter (KTU Engineering Perspective)

| Domain | Use Case |
|--------|----------|
| **E-Commerce** | Order status, offers, abandoned cart |
| **Banking/FinTech** | OTP, transaction alerts |
| **Healthcare** | Medicine reminders, lab reports |
| **Social Media** | Likes, comments, mentions |
| **IoT/Industry** | Sensor alerts, threshold breaches |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Stat (cite-with-care):** Average opt-in rate for push notifications in mobile apps is around **60–80 %** on Android, significantly higher than email open rates (≈ 20 %).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Push Notification — System Architecture

A push notification is **NOT** sent directly from your app server to the device. It travels through a **trusted platform gateway (FCM/APNs)** which authenticates and routes the message. The complete lifecycle is:

### 🔁 End-to-End Push Notification Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **App Installation / First Launch**
   - Device registers with the OS push service (e.g., FCM).
   - Receives a unique **Device Registration Token** (e.g., `cF3...xQ9`).
   - App sends this token to the **Application Backend Server** over HTTPS.

2. **Server-Initiated Push**
   - App server composes a **payload** (JSON for FCM, .apns for APNs).
   - Sends it to FCM endpoint via **HTTP v1 API** with a valid **OAuth 2.0 access token** obtained via a service account.

3. **FCM Routing**
   - FCM authenticates, validates the payload, and resolves the device token to a physical device.
   - If device is offline, FCM **queues** the message up to its TTL.

4. **Device Reception**
   - OS push service delivers payload to the client app via its registered **Service Worker / BroadcastReceiver / UNUserNotificationCenter**.

5. **Display Decision**
   - If app is in **Foreground** → custom in-app handler decides display.
   - If in **Background** or **Terminated** → OS displays automatically in notification tray.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Can't We Send Directly to Device?**
> Devices sit behind NAT, dynamic IPs, and firewalls. Persistent sockets are unreliable. FCM/APNs maintain a *single long-lived push socket per device*, dramatically reducing battery drain and complexity.

---

## 2.2 Push Notification vs In-App Messaging — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | 🔔 Push Notification | 💬 In-App Messaging |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Trigger Source** | External Server | App-internal event / SDK |
| **Internet Required** | Yes (for delivery) | Not necessarily (local rules) |
| **Displayed When App Closed** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Persistence** | Until user dismisses or expires | Until session ends or dismissed |
| **OS-Managed Display** | ✅ Yes (system tray) | ❌ No (custom UI) |
| **Channels (Android 8+)** | Required | Not applicable |
| **Reach** | Even offline users (queued) | Only active users |
| **Engagement Rate** | Medium | High (contextual) |
| **Implementation SDK** | FCM, OneSignal, Pusher | Firebase In-App Messaging, Braze |

---

## 2.3 Anatomy of an FCM Payload (KTU High-Yield Reference)

The FCM **HTTP v1 API** expects a JSON structure like:

```json
{
  "message": {
    "token": "DEVICE_REGISTRATION_TOKEN",
    "notification": {
      "title": "Order Shipped",
      "body":  "Your order #1234 is on the way!"
    },
    "data": {
      "orderId": "1234",
      "deeplink": "myapp://orders/1234"
    },
    "android": {
      "priority": "HIGH",
      "notification": {
        "channel_id": "orders_channel",
        "click_action": "OPEN_ORDERS_ACTIVITY"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Payload Tiers:**
> - `notification` → OS displays automatically (cannot be customized when app is backgrounded).
> - `data` → Custom key-value pairs delivered to app; **you** are responsible for UI rendering. Always preferred for analytics, deeplinks, and silent pushes.

---

## 2.4 Notification Channels (Mandatory Concept for KTU 2024)

From **Android 8.0 (API 26)** onwards, every notification MUST be assigned to a **channel**. Users can independently mute, override DND, or importance per channel.

### Channel Importance Levels

| Constant (Kotlin/Java) | User-Visible Effect |
|------------------------|---------------------|
| `IMPORTANCE_HIGH` | Heads-up notification + sound |
| `IMPORTANCE_DEFAULT` | Sound, no heads-up |
| `IMPORTANCE_LOW` | No sound, appears in tray |
| `IMPORTANCE_MIN` | No sound, hidden in collapsed shade |

### Channel Creation Pseudocode (Conceptual Flow)

```
Step 1 → Create a NotificationChannel(unique_id, name, importance)
Step 2 → Set description for the channel
Step 3 → Register via NotificationManager.createNotificationChannel()
Step 4 → Build NotificationCompat.Builder and set channelId
Step 5 → Notify via NotificationManagerCompat.notify(id, notification)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Creating channels in the `onCreate()` of every Activity — they should be created **once** in `Application.onCreate()` or first launch. Re-creating an existing channel throws no error but is wasteful.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Concept Reference Table)

| Concept | Definition / Rule | Mandatory From |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| **FCM** | Google's cross-platform push service | Android, iOS, Web |
| **APNs** | Apple's push service | iOS, iPadOS, macOS |
| **Device Token** | Per-app, per-device unique ID | Always required |
| **Notification Channel** | Logical grouping of notifications | Android 8.0 (API 26) |
| **POST_NOTIFICATIONS Permission** | Runtime permission to display notifications | Android 13 (API 33) |
| **Foreground Service Restriction** | Cannot start FGS while in background | Android 12+ |
| **Notification Action Buttons** | Max recommended inline actions | **3** |
| **BigTextStyle** | Multi-line expanded notification | Optional |
| **InboxStyle** | Stacked notification grouping | Optional |
| **Deeplink** | URI that opens specific app screen | `myapp://path` |
| **Silent Push** | data-only, no UI | Used for sync triggers |
| **Local Notification** | Scheduled via AlarmManager / WorkManager | No backend needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Token Lifecycle Rule (Critical for KTU):**
> Tokens can be **rotated** by FCM at any time. Always:
> 1. Listen to `onNewToken(token)` in your FirebaseMessagingService.
> 2. Re-send the new token to your backend.
> 3. Never hardcode tokens in your database permanently.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> For a mobile development course, the "derivation" is replaced with a **deterministic step-by-step implementation flow** showing how a notification moves from server to screen. Each step includes the exact component responsible and the engineering rationale.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Notification Lifecycle State Transitions

| App State | Trigger | Handler | UI Output | Who Displays? |
|-----------|---------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Foreground** | `onMessageReceived()` | Custom in-app builder | Toast / Dialog / Custom View | App developer |
| **Background** | OS intercepts `notification` field | `NotificationCompat` auto-renders | System tray banner | OS |
| **Background (data-only)** | `onMessageReceived()` | Custom builder (silent) | Optional custom UI | App developer |
| **Terminated (Killed)** | OS launches app via `click_action` | Launches launcher Activity | Notification → Activity | OS + App |
| **Terminated (Notification only)** | OS handles automatically | None | System tray banner | OS |

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Sending Your First Push Notification (Android + FCM)

### **Phase A — Project Setup (Developer Console)**

**Step 1:** Open Firebase Console → Create a new project named `KTUDemoApp`.

**Step 2:** Add an Android app to the project. Provide:
- Android package name: `in.ace.kfudemo`
- App nickname: `KTU Demo`
- Debug signing certificate SHA-1: required for OAuth login.

**Step 3:** Download `google-services.json` and place it in:
```
app/
 └── google-services.json
```

**Step 4:** Add the Google Services Gradle plugin to **project-level** `build.gradle.kts`:
```
plugins {
    id("com.google.gms.google-services") version "4.4.2" apply false
}
```

**Step 5:** Apply the plugin in **app-level** `build.gradle.kts`:
```
plugins {
    id("com.google.gms.google-services")
}

dependencies {
    implementation(platform("com.google.firebase:firebase-bom:33.5.1"))
    implementation("com.google.firebase:firebase-messaging-ktx")
}
```

> **Engineering Why:** The Firebase BoM (Bill of Materials) ensures all Firebase libs are version-aligned, preventing classpath conflicts.

---

### **Phase B — Service Declaration in Manifest**

**Step 6:** Add the FCM service declaration inside `AndroidManifest.xml`:
```xml
<service
    android:name=".MyFirebaseMessagingService"
    android:exported="false">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="com.google.firebase.MESSAGING_EVENT" />
    </intent-filter>
</service>
```

> **Why `exported="false"`?**
> From Android 12 (API 31) onwards, services with intent filters must explicitly declare exported status. Internal FCM services should NOT be exposed to other apps.

---

### **Phase C — Implementing the Messaging Service (Kotlin)**

**Step 7:** Create `MyFirebaseMessagingService.kt`:
```kotlin
class MyFirebaseMessagingService : FirebaseMessagingService() {

    override fun onNewToken(token: String) {
        super.onNewToken(token)
        Log.d("FCM", "New token: $token")
        // TODO: Send token to your application backend
    }

    override fun onMessageReceived(message: RemoteMessage) {
        super.onMessageReceived(message)
        val title = message.notification?.title ?: "KTU Demo"
        val body  = message.notification?.body ?: "New notification"
        val data  = message.data
        showNotification(title, body, data)
    }

    private fun showNotification(title: String, body: String, data: Map<String, String>) {
        // (a) Create channel
        val channelId = "default_channel"
        if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.O) {
            val channel = NotificationChannel(
                channelId,
                "Default Channel",
                NotificationManager.IMPORTANCE_HIGH
            ).apply { description = "General notifications" }
            getSystemService(NotificationManager::class.java)
                .createNotificationChannel(channel)
        }

        // (b) Build intent (deeplink)
        val intent = Intent(this, MainActivity::class.java).apply {
            flags = Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP
            data = Uri.parse(data["deeplink"] ?: "myapp://home")
        }
        val pendingIntent = PendingIntent.getActivity(
            this, 0, intent,
            PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE
        )

        // (c) Build notification
        val notification = NotificationCompat.Builder(this, channelId)
            .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_notification)
            .setContentTitle(title)
            .setContentText(body)
            .setAutoCancel(true)
            .setContentIntent(pendingIntent)
            .setPriority(NotificationCompat.PRIORITY_HIGH)
            .build()

        NotificationManagerCompat.from(this).notify(1, notification)
    }
}
```

---

### **Phase D — Server-Side Push via FCM HTTP v1 API**

**Step 8:** Generate an OAuth 2.0 access token using a service account (via Firebase Admin SDK or REST):
```
POST https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer&assertion={SIGNED_JWT}
```

**Step 9:** Send the FCM message:
```
POST https://fcm.googleapis.com/v1/projects/kfudemo/messages:send
Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS_TOKEN}
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "message": {
    "token": "DEVICE_REGISTRATION_TOKEN",
    "notification": { "title": "Hello KTU", "body": "Module 3 Demo" },
    "data": { "deeplink": "myapp://home" }
  }
}
```

**Step 10:** Verify response status `200 OK` with a `name` field like:
```json
{ "name": "projects/kfudemo/messages/0:1717000000000%abc" }
```

> **Engineering Insight:** A failed push returns HTTP 404 if the token is invalid (e.g., app uninstalled). Your backend MUST delete such tokens to prevent retry storms.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Reasoning — Why In-App Messaging Is Different

In-App Messaging is **client-driven**. There is **no server push**. The SDK uses a combination of:

1. **Local event listeners** — fires when user opens app, logs in, or completes an event.
2. **Cached campaign bundles** — fetched periodically from Firebase servers when online.
3. **Display eligibility rules** — frequency caps, audience targeting, contextual triggers.

### Step-by-Step: Implementing Firebase In-App Messaging

**Step 1:** Add dependency:
```
implementation("com.google.firebase:firebase-inappmessaging-display-ktx")
```

**Step 2:** The SDK auto-initialises. No manifest changes required.

**Step 3:** Create a campaign in Firebase Console → Engage → In-App Messaging.

**Step 4:** Programmatically trigger via event:
```kotlin
FirebaseInAppMessaging.getInstance().triggerEvent("order_placed")
```

**Step 5:** To customise appearance (e.g., background colour), implement `FirebaseInAppMessagingDisplay` and override `displayMessage(...)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is In-App Messaging higher-converting?**
> Because it appears *only* to active users in *contextual* moments (e.g., after checkout), avoiding the noise penalty that push notifications suffer from.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended Approach | Justification |
|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| Daily deal alert | **Push (FCM)** | Reach inactive users |
| "Welcome to the app" tour | **In-App** | Show only on first launch |
| Reminder: complete your profile | **Push after 24 h** | Re-engagement |
| Real-time chat message | **Push + In-App deeplink** | Notify + open chat |
| Silent data sync | **Data-only push** | Wake app to fetch data |
| Medicine reminder at 8 PM daily | **Local Notification** | No backend required |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Push Notification Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Device
    participant App as Mobile App
    participant FCM as FCM Service
    participant Server as App Backend Server

    Note over U,App: First-time App Launch
    App->>FCM: Request device token
    FCM-->>App: Returns registration token (cF3...xQ9)
    App->>Server: POST /register {token: "cF3...xQ9"}

    Note over Server,FCM: Later - Server decides to notify user
    Server->>FCM: POST /v1/messages:send {token, payload}
    FCM->>U: Delivers via persistent push socket
    U->>App: onMessageReceived() or OS auto-display
    App->>U: Renders notification in tray / custom UI
    U->>App: User taps notification
    App->>App: Opens deeplink target Activity
```

## 4.2 Push vs In-App Messaging — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Backend[Application Backend Server]
        API[REST API Server]
        DB[(User Database)]
        API --- DB
    end

    subgraph Cloud[Google Cloud / Apple Push Infrastructure]
        FCM[Firebase Cloud Messaging FCM]
        APN[Apple Push Notification Service APNs]
    end

    subgraph Device[Mobile Device]
        direction TB
        OS[Operating System Push Layer]
        FG[Foreground State]
        BG[Background State]
        TR[Terminated State]
        OS --> FG
        OS --> BG
        OS --> TR
    end

    subgraph Channels[Android Notification Channels]
        C1[Orders Channel HIGH]
        C2[Promo Channel DEFAULT]
        C3[Alerts Channel MIN]
    end

    API -->|HTTPS POST| FCM
    API -->|HTTPS POST| APN
    FCM -->|Push Socket| OS
    APN -->|Push Socket| OS
    FG --> Channels
    BG --> Channels
    TR --> Channels

    InApp[In-App Messaging SDK]
    InApp -->|Triggers on event| FG
```

## 4.3 Notification Payload Processing — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming FCM Payload] --> B{Contains notification field?}
    B -- Yes --> C{App State?}
    B -- No --> D[Silent Data Push]
    C -- Foreground --> E[Custom in app handler decides display]
    C -- Background --> F[OS renders system tray banner]
    C -- Terminated --> G[OS launches entry Activity then displays]
    D --> H[App processes data silently]
    E --> I[Channel ID Lookup]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> J[Custom Sync Logic]
    I --> K[NotificationManagerCompat.notify]
```

## 4.4 Notification Channel Architecture (Android 8+)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    App[Application Context] --> NM[NotificationManager]
    NM --> CH1[Channel orders_channel HIGH]
    NM --> CH2[Channel promo_channel DEFAULT]
    NM --> CH3[Channel system_channel MIN]
    CH1 --> U1[User can independently mute]
    CH2 --> U2[User can override DND]
    CH3 --> U3[User can hide in collapsed shade]
    U1 --> N1[Order notifications]
    U2 --> N2[Promotional banners]
    U3 --> N3[Background sync alerts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:**
> - Rectangular blocks represent software components.
> - Arrows represent data/control flow direction.
> - Subgraph boundaries represent logical trust/runtime boundaries.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Push Notifications and In-App Messaging. Mention any two real-world use cases for each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Push Notifications** are server-initiated messages delivered via platform push services (FCM/APNs) and displayed by the OS even when the app is closed.
  - *Use cases:* Banking transaction alerts; cab arrival notifications.
- **In-App Messaging** is UI-rendered, client-driven messaging shown only during an active app session.
  - *Use cases:* "Welcome to the app" first-launch tour; checkout upsell banner.
- **Key distinction:** Push works across app lifecycle states (foreground/background/terminated); In-App works only in foreground.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why are Notification Channels mandatory from Android 8.0? List any four importance levels.**

**Model Answer:**
Notification Channels were introduced in **Android 8.0 (API 26)** to give users **granular control** over notification categories. Apps must declare channels; users can independently configure sound, vibration, and DND override per channel.

**Four Importance Levels:**
1. `IMPORTANCE_HIGH` — Heads-up + sound
2. `IMPORTANCE_DEFAULT` — Sound only
3. `IMPORTANCE_LOW` — Silent, in tray
4. `IMPORTANCE_MIN` — Silent, hidden

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **FCM token** with **Firebase project ID**. They are different — token is per-device per-app; project ID is global.
> - In Android 13+, missing the `POST_NOTIFICATIONS` runtime permission results in **silently dropped notifications**. Always declare AND request at runtime.
> - Setting `channelId` in the builder without creating the channel first causes the notification to **fail silently** with no crash — hard to debug. Always create channels BEFORE building.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete architecture of an FCM-based push notification system with a neat block diagram. List the responsibilities of each component.

**Model Solution:**

| Component | Responsibility |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Mobile App (Client)** | Registers for push, receives token, sends token to backend, handles `onMessageReceived`. |
| **Application Backend Server** | Stores device tokens, composes payload, authenticates with FCM, sends via REST API. |
| **Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM)** | Authenticates server, routes message to correct device, queues offline messages per TTL, maintains persistent socket. |
| **Device OS Push Layer** | Receives payload, decides display based on app state and channel, handles user tap → deeplink. |
| **Notification Channels** | Logical groupings for user-controlled importance, sound, DND behaviour. |

**Block Diagram:**
```
[App Server] ──HTTPS──▶ [FCM Gateway] ──Persistent Socket──▶ [Device OS] ──▶ [App / Tray]
      ▲                                                                │
      └──────Token Registration────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the step-by-step procedure to implement push notifications in an Android application using Firebase Cloud Messaging. Include manifest configuration and the essential Kotlin code for the messaging service.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Create Firebase project, add Android app with package name, download `google-services.json`.

**Step 2:** Apply plugin and add dependencies in Gradle:
```
plugins { id("com.google.gms.google-services") }
implementation(platform("com.google.firebase:firebase-bom:33.5.1"))
implementation("com.google.firebase:firebase-messaging-ktx")
```

**Step 3:** Declare service in `AndroidManifest.xml`:
```xml
<service
    android:name=".MyFirebaseMessagingService"
    android:exported="false">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="com.google.firebase.MESSAGING_EVENT" />
    </intent-filter>
</service>
```

**Step 4:** Implement `FirebaseMessagingService` subclass (as shown in Section 3.2, Step 7 of these notes) with `onNewToken()` and `onMessageReceived()` overrides.

**Step 5:** Send push from backend using FCM HTTP v1 API with OAuth 2.0 access token.

**Step 6 (Android 13+):** Request `POST_NOTIFICATIONS` runtime permission in the launcher Activity.

**Engineering Notes:**
- Always override `onNewToken()` because FCM rotates tokens without notice.
- Use **data-only** payload when you need to render custom UI in foreground.
- Use **notification+data** when background OS display is acceptable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Push Notifications with In-App Messaging. For each type, explain two scenarios where it is the optimal choice with justification.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Push Notification | In-App Messaging |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Lifecycle reach | Foreground + Background + Terminated | Foreground only |
| Trigger | Server push | Local event / SDK |
| Display owner | OS (system tray) | App (custom UI) |
| Conversion rate | Medium (noise penalty) | High (contextual) |
| Implementation effort | Backend + Client | Client only |

**Optimal Scenarios:**
1. **Re-engagement campaign** (e.g., 30-day inactive user) → **Push**, because user is unlikely to have app open.
2. **"Welcome tour" on first launch** → **In-App**, because user is already active and contextual timing matters.
3. **Order delivery update** → **Push with deeplink**, because user must be informed anywhere, anytime.
4. **"Rate our app" prompt after 3 successful orders** → **In-App**, because event-driven and contextual.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Demonstrate with Kotlin code how to: (i) Create a Notification Channel for high-importance alerts, (ii) Build and display a notification with a deeplink intent, (iii) Schedule a local notification using WorkManager.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Create Notification Channel:**
```kotlin
if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.O) {
    val channel = NotificationChannel(
        "alerts_channel",
        "High Priority Alerts",
        NotificationManager.IMPORTANCE_HIGH
    ).apply { description = "Urgent user notifications" }
    val nm = getSystemService(NotificationManager::class.java)
    nm.createNotificationChannel(channel)
}
```

**(ii) Build and Display with Deeplink:**
```kotlin
val intent = Intent(
    Intent.ACTION_VIEW,
    Uri.parse("myapp://orders/1234"),
    this,
    OrderDetailActivity::class.java
)
val pi = PendingIntent.getActivity(
    this, 0, intent,
    PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT or PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE
)
val notif = NotificationCompat.Builder(this, "alerts_channel")
    .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_alert)
    .setContentTitle("Order Delivered")
    .setContentText("Tap to view details")
    .setContentIntent(pi)
    .setAutoCancel(true)
    .setPriority(NotificationCompat.PRIORITY_HIGH)
    .build()
NotificationManagerCompat.from(this).notify(101, notif)
```

**(iii) Schedule Local Notification (WorkManager):**
```kotlin
class NotifyWorker(ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters) : Worker(ctx, params) {
    override fun doWork(): Result {
        // Same builder code as above
        NotificationManagerCompat.from(applicationContext).notify(202, notif)
        return Result.success()
    }
}

// Schedule
val request = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<NotifyWorker>()
    .setInitialDelay(30, TimeUnit.MINUTES)
    .build()
WorkManager.getInstance(this).enqueue(request)
```

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Push** = server-driven, OS-rendered, works in all app states.
- ✅ **In-App** = client-driven, UI-rendered, foreground only.
- ✅ FCM payload has **two fields**: `notification` (OS-displayable) and `data` (custom key-value).
- ✅ FCM HTTP v1 API requires **OAuth 2.0** access token, not legacy server key.
- ✅ **Device tokens must be refreshed** — always override `onNewToken()`.
- ✅ **Notification Channels are mandatory from Android 8.0 (API 26)**.
- ✅ **POST_NOTIFICATIONS runtime permission** is mandatory from **Android 13 (API 33)**.
- ✅ **Foreground notifications are NOT auto-displayed** unless explicitly built.
- ✅ `PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE` is **required from Android 12 (API 31)**.
- ✅ In-App Messaging SDK is auto-initialised; **no manifest entry required**.
- ✅ Use **data-only** payload when you need custom rendering or silent sync.
- ✅ Use **deeplinks** (`myapp://path`) to route users from notification to specific screen.
- ✅ **BigTextStyle**, **InboxStyle**, **MediaStyle** enhance notification richness.
- ✅ FCM message payload limit is **4096 bytes (4 KB)**.
- ✅ `onMessageReceived()` is invoked only when app is in foreground OR when payload is data-only.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PUSH DAILY":**
> **P**ayload (notification + data) • **U**ser permission (Android 13) • **S**ervice declared in manifest • **H**TTP v1 + OAuth • **D**eeplink intent • **A**pp state handling (FG/BG/TR) • **I**mportance levels (4) • **L**ocal vs Remote • **Y**our token lifecycle.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 3 — IMPLEMENTING PUSH NOTIFICATIONS AND IN-APP MESSAGING**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Compliant*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lab: Setting up and Integrating a Firebase Backend with a Mobile App
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT744 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lab: Setting up and Integrating a Firebase Backend with a Mobile App

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Firebase** is a Backend-as-a-Service (BaaS) platform developed by Google that provides cloud-hosted backend services such as authentication, real-time databases, cloud storage, hosting, and serverless functions for mobile and web applications. In the context of the **PEITT744 Mobile App Development** laboratory, Firebase is integrated with a mobile client (Android/iOS/Flutter) to enable server-side capabilities without manually provisioning servers, writing REST APIs, or managing infrastructure.

A **Firebase Backend** refers to the collection of cloud services initialized via the Firebase Console, configured through `google-services.json` (Android) or `GoogleService-Info.plist` (iOS), and accessed programmatically through the Firebase SDK.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Lab):** The lab exercise expects students to demonstrate end-to-end integration of Firebase — project creation, SDK integration, Firebase Authentication, Cloud Firestore/Realtime Database CRUD operations, and verifying data persistence through the Firebase Console.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a mobile app like a personal diary. Without a backend, your diary entries live only on your phone — if you switch devices, you lose everything. Now imagine **Firebase as a magical, invisible notebook that lives in the cloud**:

- Whenever you write a new diary entry on your phone, a **ghost copy** is instantly saved in Google's cloud.
- When you log in from a new device, the **ghost notebook hands over all your previous entries**.
- You don't need to build a warehouse to store the notebook, hire a librarian to manage it, or pay for a courier to send it — Google does all of that for free (within limits).

That's exactly what Firebase does for your mobile app: it provides a **ready-made, scalable, real-time backend** that you "plug in" using a configuration file and a few lines of code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Firebase:**
> - **Free Tier (Spark Plan):** 10K Authentication verifications/month, 1 GiB Firestore storage, 10 GB/month bandwidth.
> - **Realtime Database Latency:** Typically **< 100 ms** for regional reads.
> - **Firestore SLA Uptime:** **99.999%** on the Blaze plan for multi-region deployments.

## 1.3 Why Firebase for Mobile Apps?

| Feature | Benefit to Mobile Developer |
|---|---|
| **Authentication** | Pre-built UI for Email, Google, Phone, OTP login |
| **Cloud Firestore** | NoSQL document-based real-time database |
| **Realtime Database** | JSON tree-based DB with WebSocket sync |
| **Cloud Storage** | Store user-uploaded images, videos, PDFs |
| **Cloud Messaging (FCM)** | Push notifications to devices |
| **Crashlytics** | Real-time crash reporting |
| **Hosting** | Deploy web assets with one command |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Firebase-Integrated Mobile App

A Firebase-integrated mobile application follows a **Client–Cloud–Console** three-tier architecture:

1. **Client Tier (Mobile App):** The Android/iOS/Flutter application that interacts with the user.
2. **Cloud Tier (Firebase Services):** Google's managed infrastructure hosting Auth, Firestore, Storage, Functions.
3. **Console Tier (Firebase Console):** A web-based dashboard for project management, rule configuration, analytics, and data inspection.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Project Creation:** A Firebase project is a top-level container that groups all cloud resources for an app. Created via the Firebase Console or programmatically via the Firebase Management REST API.
- **Step 2 — App Registration:** The mobile app is registered to the Firebase project using its package name (Android) or Bundle ID (iOS). This binds the app identity to the project.
- **Step 3 — Configuration File Download:** A `google-services.json` (Android) or `GoogleService-Info.plist` (iOS) file is downloaded. This file contains API keys, project ID, and storage bucket details.
- **Step 4 — SDK Integration:** The Firebase SDK (libraries) is added to the mobile app project (via Gradle for Android, CocoaPods for iOS, pubspec.yaml for Flutter).
- **Step 5 — Initialization:** On app launch, the SDK reads the configuration file and authenticates the app with Google's servers.
- **Step 6 — Service Usage:** The app calls Firebase APIs (e.g., `FirebaseAuth.getInstance().signInWithEmailAndPassword(...)`).
- **Step 7 — Data Persistence:** Data is stored in Firestore / Realtime Database and can be inspected in real time via the Firebase Console.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Term | Definition | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Firebase Project** | Top-level container for all Firebase services for one app | Often asked: "What is a Firebase project?" |
| **google-services.json** | Configuration file linking Android app to Firebase project | Asked in lab viva: "Where do you place this file?" |
| **Cloud Firestore** | NoSQL, document-oriented, real-time database | High-yield for CRUD-based lab questions |
| **Realtime Database** | NoSQL, JSON tree, low-latency, WebSocket-based | Often compared with Firestore |
| **Firebase Authentication** | Service providing identity verification | Login/signup lab question staple |
| **Security Rules** | Server-side validation rules for Firestore/RTDB | Viva favorite: "Explain Firestore rules syntax." |
| **Firebase SDK** | Software Development Kit — libraries for client integration | "List three Firebase SDK packages." |
| **Collection / Document** | Firestore data model: Collections contain documents | Essential for data modeling questions |
| **FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging)** | Push notification service | Frequently tested in notifications module |
| **Crashlytics** | Crash analytics and reporting tool | Bonus viva question |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in real engineering:** Firebase eliminates the need for DevOps infrastructure. Startups ship MVPs (Minimum Viable Products) in days rather than months. Companies like **Alibaba, The New York Times, and Venmo** use Firebase in production for real-time features.

## 2.3 Comparison: Firestore vs Realtime Database

| Property | Cloud Firestore | Realtime Database |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Documents & Collections | Flat JSON Tree |
| Querying | Rich compound queries | Limited queries |
| Offline Support | Yes (built-in) | Yes (built-in) |
| Scalability | Horizontal (sharded) | Single region |
| Pricing | Per-document read/write | Per GB downloaded |
| Best For | Complex hierarchical data | Simple, low-latency sync |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Lab Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Lab Objective

**Goal:** Set up a Firebase project, integrate it with an Android mobile app (using Java/Kotlin or Flutter), implement user authentication (Email/Password), and perform Create-Read-Update-Delete (CRUD) operations on **Cloud Firestore**.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure (Android — Kotlin)

### Step A — Create a Firebase Project

1. Open [https://console.firebase.google.com](https://console.firebase.google.com).
2. Click **"Add Project"** → Enter project name (e.g., `KTU-Peitt744-Lab`) → Continue.
3. Enable/disable Google Analytics → **Create Project**.
4. Once created, click the **Android icon** to register an Android app.

### Step B — Register the Android App

- Enter **Android package name** (e.g., `com.example.ktufirebaselab`).
- Optionally provide a nickname and debug signing certificate SHA-1.
- Click **Register App**.

### Step C — Download `google-services.json`

- Place the downloaded `google-services.json` file into the **app-level** directory:

```
MyApplication/
├── app/
│   ├── google-services.json   ← Place here
│   ├── src/
│   └── build.gradle.kts
└── build.gradle.kts
```

### Step D — Add Firebase SDK to Gradle

In the **project-level** `build.gradle.kts`:

```kotlin
plugins {
    id("com.android.application") version "8.1.0" apply false
    id("org.jetbrains.kotlin.android") version "1.9.0" apply false
    id("com.google.gms.google-services") version "4.4.0" apply false
}
```

In the **app-level** `build.gradle.kts`:

```kotlin
plugins {
    id("com.android.application")
    id("org.jetbrains.kotlin.android")
    id("com.google.gms.google-services")
}

dependencies {
    implementation(platform("com.google.firebase:firebase-bom:32.6.0"))
    implementation("com.google.firebase:firebase-auth-ktx")
    implementation("com.google.firebase:firebase-firestore-ktx")
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Firebase BoM (Bill of Materials)** ensures all Firebase libraries are version-compatible automatically.

### Step E — Enable Authentication in Firebase Console

1. In Firebase Console → **Authentication** → **Get Started**.
2. Enable **Email/Password** sign-in method.

### Step F — Enable Firestore Database

1. Firebase Console → **Firestore Database** → **Create Database**.
2. Choose **Start in Test Mode** (for lab purposes) → Select region (e.g., `eur3` or `asia-south1`).
3. Click **Enable**.

### Step G — Implement User Registration (Kotlin)

```kotlin
import com.google.firebase.auth.FirebaseAuth
import com.google.firebase.auth.ktx.auth
import com.google.firebase.ktx.Firebase

class AuthManager {
    private val auth: FirebaseAuth = Firebase.auth

    fun registerUser(email: String, password: String, onResult: (Boolean, String) -> Unit) {
        auth.createUserWithEmailAndPassword(email, password)
            .addOnCompleteListener { task ->
                if (task.isSuccessful) {
                    onResult(true, "Registration Successful: ${auth.currentUser?.uid}")
                } else {
                    onResult(false, "Error: ${task.exception?.message}")
                }
            }
    }

    fun loginUser(email: String, password: String, onResult: (Boolean, String) -> Unit) {
        auth.signInWithEmailAndPassword(email, password)
            .addOnCompleteListener { task ->
                if (task.isSuccessful) {
                    onResult(true, "Login Successful")
                } else {
                    onResult(false, "Error: ${task.exception?.message}")
                }
            }
    }
}
```

### Step H — Implement Firestore CRUD

```kotlin
import com.google.firebase.firestore.FirebaseFirestore
import com.google.firebase.firestore.ktx.firestore
import com.google.firebase.ktx.Firebase

class FirestoreManager {
    private val db: FirebaseFirestore = Firebase.firestore

    // CREATE
    fun addNote(userId: String, title: String, content: String) {
        val note = hashMapOf(
            "title" to title,
            "content" to content,
            "timestamp" to System.currentTimeMillis()
        )
        db.collection("users").document(userId)
            .collection("notes")
            .add(note)
    }

    // READ
    fun getNotes(userId: String, onResult: (List<Map<String, Any>>) -> Unit) {
        db.collection("users").document(userId)
            .collection("notes")
            .get()
            .addOnSuccessListener { result ->
                val notes = result.documents.map { it.data ?: emptyMap() }
                onResult(notes)
            }
    }

    // UPDATE
    fun updateNote(userId: String, noteId: String, newTitle: String) {
        db.collection("users").document(userId)
            .collection("notes").document(noteId)
            .update("title", newTitle)
    }

    // DELETE
    fun deleteNote(userId: String, noteId: String) {
        db.collection("users").document(userId)
            .collection("notes").document(noteId)
            .delete()
    }
}
```

### Step I — Basic Firestore Security Rules

```javascript
rules_version = '2';
service cloud.firestore {
  match /databases/{database}/documents {
    match /users/{userId}/{document=**} {
      allow read, write: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == userId;
    }
  }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- `request.auth != null` ensures only logged-in users can access data.
- `request.auth.uid == userId` ensures a user can only access their own data.

### Step J — Verify in Firebase Console

1. Navigate to **Authentication → Users** to verify registered accounts.
2. Navigate to **Firestore Database → Data** to verify the stored notes appear in real time.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Lab Errors and Fixes

| Error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `google-services.json not found` | File in wrong directory | Place in `app/` folder |
| `Default FirebaseApp is not initialized` | Missing plugin | Add `id("com.google.gms.google-services")` to app build.gradle |
| `PERMISSION_DENIED` on Firestore read | Security rules blocking | Update rules in Firebase Console |
| `EMAIL_ALREADY_IN_USE` | Duplicate registration | Use a different email or login flow |
| `Network error` | No internet or wrong API key | Re-download `google-services.json` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End Firebase Integration Flow

