# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Debugging and Profiling in mobile application development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Debugging** is the process of identifying and fixing *functional* (logic) errors in code using breakpoints, watches, and step-execution. It answers *"Is the app doing the right thing?"* (1 mark)
- **Profiling** is the process of measuring *non-functional* runtime metrics (CPU, memory, network, energy) under realistic workloads. It answers *"Is the app doing the right thing fast enough and with acceptable resource use?"* (1 mark)
- **Key difference:** Debugging uses the **JDWP / LLDB** protocol and pauses execution; Profiling uses **sampling / instrumentation** and aims to run with minimal overhead to capture true production-like behavior. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the four sub-tools of Android Studio Profiler and write the purpose of any two.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. CPU Profiler
2. Memory Profiler
3. Network Profiler
4. Energy Profiler
- **CPU Profiler purpose:** Records method call execution time on each thread, identifies hot methods and main-thread blocking. (1 mark)
- **Memory Profiler purpose:** Visualizes live memory, captures Java/native heap dumps, and helps locate memory leaks and excessive allocations. (1 mark)
- (Any two purposes at 1 mark each; the 1 remaining mark is for the list.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Q3. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
### (A) — Choice 1

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Android Studio Profiler with a neat diagram. Discuss how the CPU Profiler helps in identifying main-thread bottlenecks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a step-by-step procedure, describe how you would use Xcode Instruments (Time Profiler + Allocations) to diagnose and fix a scroll-jank issue in a `UITableView` displaying remote images. **(7 marks)**

### (B) — Choice 2

**(a)** Define "memory leak" in the context of Android. Explain how LeakCanary works and write a code snippet showing a common leak scenario and its fix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is "Energy Impact Score" in Xcode Energy Log? List four major contributors to high energy consumption and the optimization for each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q3 (A) — Choice 1

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture of Android Studio Profiler (4 marks):**
The Android Studio Profiler is a real-time, multi-pane observability tool integrated into the IDE. It communicates with the running app process on a connected device/emulator through a **JDWP-based debug bridge** and uses **ART (Android Runtime) hooks** to sample execution without significantly slowing the app.

**The four coordinated panes are:**

1. **CPU Pane** — Samples the call stack at a configurable rate (default ~1 kHz). Three modes: *Sampled* (statistical), *Instrumented* (precise via bytecode rewriting), and *System Trace* (kernel + choreographer events).
2. **Memory Pane** — Shows Java heap, native heap, graphics, and code segments in a stacked area chart. Supports manual **Heap Dump** to `.hprof` and **Allocation Tracking** with call-stack attribution.
3. **Network Pane** — Intercepts HTTP/HTTPS requests via the `OkHttp` EventListener API (or platform hooks) and displays sent/received bytes, status, and timeline.
4. **Energy Pane** — Monitors Wakelocks, AlarmManager, JobScheduler, GPS, and sensor usage. Uses a color-coded energy estimate (Low / Medium / High).

**Identifying Main-Thread Bottlenecks (3 marks):**
- Open the CPU Profiler in **System Trace** mode. Locate the **Choreographer.doFrame** entry on the main thread.
- If a frame slice exceeds **16.67 ms** (the 60 FPS budget), the corresponding slice is drawn as a **red bar** (jank marker).
- The **Call Chart** or **Flame Chart** beneath shows which method consumed the time. Common offenders: `BitmapFactory.decodeStream`, `JSONObject(string)`, disk I/O.
- **Fix pattern:** Move the offending call to a background thread (`Dispatchers.IO` in Kotlin, `ThreadPoolExecutor` in Java) and post the result back to the main thread.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step-by-step Xcode Instruments Diagnosis of `UITableView` Jank (7 marks):**

1. **Build in Profile mode:** Product → Profile (⌘I). Select the **Time Profiler + Allocations** template pair. (1 mark)
2. **Record baseline:** Click Record, scroll the `UITableView` for 10 seconds, stop. (1 mark)
3. **Read the Time Profiler call tree:** Switch to **Top Functions**, enable "Separate by Thread" and "Hide System Libraries". The widest bottom-of-flame node reveals the hot method — typically `ImageIO` JPEG decoding or `UIImage(contentsOfFile:)` initialization on the main thread. (1 mark)
4. **Read Allocations:** Switch to the Allocations instrument. Set **Display Settings → All Heap & Anonymous VM Regions**. Persistent bytes that grow with each scroll indicate an **unbounded image cache**. (1 mark)
5. **Confirm with a second trace:** Use the **Inspect** tool to mark a specific frame; the instruments will show the exact code path that produced the slow frame. (1 mark)
6. **Apply the fix — Image Downsampling off the main thread:** (2 marks)

```swift
// Inside cellForRowAt
DispatchQueue.global(qos: .userInitiated).async {
    guard let url = URL(string: imageURL) else { return }
    guard let data = try? Data(contentsOf: url) else { return }
    let options: [CFString: Any] = [
        kCGImageSourceCreateThumbnailFromImageAlways: true,
        kCGImageSourceShouldCacheImmediately: true,
        kCGImageSourceCreateThumbnailWithTransform: true,
        kCGImageSourceThumbnailMaxPixelSize: 200
    ]
    let cgImage = CGImageSourceCreateThumbnailAtIndex(
        CGImageSourceCreateWithData(data as CFData, nil)!, 0, options as CFDictionary)
    let downsampled = UIImage(cgImage: cgImage!)
    DispatchQueue.main.async {
        cell.imageView?.image = downsampled
    }
}
```

7. **Re-record:** Verify that Self Weight of `ImageIO` drops below 1% and FPS stabilizes at 60.

---

### Model Solution — Q3 (B) — Choice 2

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Memory Leak Definition (1 mark):**
A *memory leak* in Android is a situation where objects that are no longer needed by the application remain reachable through accidental strong references (e.g., inner classes, static fields, registered listeners), preventing the **Garbage Collector** from reclaiming their memory.

**How LeakCanary Works (3 marks):**
- LeakCanary is a **debug-only** memory leak detection library by Square.
- It hooks into the `Activity` and `Fragment` lifecycles via `Application.ActivityLifecycleCallbacks`.
- After `Activity.onDestroy()`, it schedules a heap dump via `ReferenceQueue` + `WeakReference`.
- It then runs the heap through a **retainment tree analysis** (using the `HAHA` library) to find the GC root that is keeping the destroyed object alive.
- If a leak is detected, a **heads-up notification** with a full leak trace is shown to the developer.

**Leak Scenario (1.5 marks):**

```java
public class LeakyActivity extends AppCompatActivity {
    private static Context sContext; // STATIC reference!
    @Override protected void onCreate(Bundle s) {
        super.onCreate(s);
        sContext = this; // LEAK: activity survives process lifetime
    }
}
```

**Fix (1.5 marks):**

```java
public class FixedActivity extends AppCompatActivity {
    @Override protected void onCreate(Bundle s) {
        super.onCreate(s);
        // Use ApplicationContext for long-lived singletons
        MySingleton.init(getApplicationContext());
    }
}
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Energy Impact Score Definition (1 mark):**
The **Energy Impact Score** in Xcode Energy Log is a normalized 0–20 rating of an app's total energy consumption over the recorded period. A score of **0** indicates negligible drain; a score of **20** indicates the app is among the worst energy offenders in the system.

**Four Major Energy Contributors and Their Optimizations (6 marks, 1.5 each):**

1. **High CPU usage on the main thread** (e.g., tight loops, polling).
   - *Fix:* Use `DispatchQueue.global(qos: .utility)` for deferrable work; enable **App Nap** by not opting out unnecessarily.

2. **Frequent network calls / radio wakeups**.
   - *Fix:* Use **URLSessionConfiguration with `waitsForConnectivity = true`** and **background transfers** so the system can batch network radio wake-ups.

3. **Excessive Core Location updates** at the highest accuracy.
   - *Fix:* Use `desiredAccuracy = kCLLocationAccuracyHundredMeters` or `kCLLocationAccuracyThreeKilometers` for non-navigation apps; call `stopUpdatingLocation()` once the data is acquired. Use **Significant-Change Service** instead of continuous updates.

4. **Continuous background animations / high-refresh display usage**.
   - *Fix:* Pause animations when the app is backgrounded (`applicationWillResignActive`); reduce `preferredFrameRateRange`; use `CADisplayLink` only when actively animating.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse **Energy Profiler (Android)** with **Energy Log (iOS)** — they have different scoring scales and metric sets.
> - **Do not** claim that `static` variables in Java/Kotlin are *always* leaks. They are leaks **only if** they capture a lifecycle-bound object (Activity/Fragment/View) for longer than the process.
> - **Always** re-profile *after* a fix. KTU specifically tests for the *iterative* nature of the workflow. A single screenshot of a profiler with no follow-up fix is a 0–1 mark answer on a 7-mark sub-question.
> - **Do not** say "use `finalize()`" to fix leaks — `finalize()` is deprecated in Java 9+ and disallowed in Swift's ARC entirely.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ **The 5 Profiling Axes:** CPU, Memory, Network, Energy/Battery, UI Rendering.
- ✅ **The 60 FPS Frame Budget:** **16.67 ms** per frame — anything more is **jank**.
- ✅ **Cold-start components:** Process fork + Activity init + First frame draw.
- ✅ **Memory Leak Triad:** (1) Object no longer needed, (2) Still strongly referenced, (3) GC cannot reclaim.
- ✅ **LeakCanary Trigger:** Hooks `onDestroy()` → forces GC → dumps heap → analyzes retain path.
- ✅ **Xcode Energy Log Score Range:** **0 (best) → 20 (worst)**.
- ✅ **Android Studio Profiler access path:** Run → **Profile 'app'** (NOT Debug).
- ✅ **Xcode Instruments access path:** Product → **Profile** (⌘I) (NOT Run).
- ✅ **Main-Thread Rule:** Heavy work > 16 ms → move off main thread.
- ✅ **Allocation Budget:** < **0.5 MB/sec** for smooth scrolling.
- ✅ **Top 4 Android Bottlenecks:** Image decoding, JSON parsing, disk I/O, deep view hierarchy.
- ✅ **Top 4 iOS Bottlenecks:** `UIImage` decode, Auto Layout solve, CoreText layout, CoreData main-thread fetch.
- ✅ **The Iterative Loop:** Profile → Diagnose → Fix → **Re-Profile** to verify.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked *"how would you optimize a mobile app"*, **always** start your answer with a *measurement* (profiler data), not a guess. This single sentence earns you the examiner's first impression marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lab: Implementing Secure Coding Practices and Encryption in Mobile Apps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT744 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Lab: Implementing Secure Coding Practices and Encryption in Mobile Apps # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Secure Coding Practices** in mobile application development refer to a structured set of guidelines, principles, and defensive programming techniques that are applied during the software development lifecycle (SDLC) to prevent the introduction of security vulnerabilities, protect sensitive data, and ensure the application behaves securely even when subjected to malicious inputs, reverse engineering, man-in-the-middle attacks, or unauthorized access attempts. **Encryption in Mobile Apps** is the cryptographic process of transforming readable plaintext data (such as user credentials, personal information, financial data, or session tokens) into an unreadable ciphertext format using mathematical algorithms and cryptographic keys, ensuring that the data remains confidential, integral, and authentic during storage (data-at-rest) and transmission (data-in-transit). As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEITT744 – Module 4)**, this lab exercise trains students in: - Implementing **input validation** and **output encoding** - Applying **principle of least privilege** to permissions - Utilizing platform-provided **secure storage APIs** (Keychain on iOS, Keystore on Android) - Implementing **cryptographic primitives** — symmetric encryption (AES), asymmetric encryption (RSA), and one-way hashing (SHA-256) - Defending against the **OWASP Mobile Top 10** vulnerabilities > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > *"Lab: Implementing secure coding practices and encryption in mobile apps — Students are expected to demonstrate the use of hashing (SHA), symmetric (AES) and asymmetric (RSA) encryption, secure storage of credentials, and the application of OWASP Mobile Top 10 mitigation strategies."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sending a **sealed, tamper-proof diary** to your friend across a busy city: 1. **The Diary (Plaintext)** — Your personal thoughts written openly. Anyone who finds the diary can read it. 2. **The Lock (Encryption Algorithm)** — A special mechanical lock with a mathematical key. The lock scrambles the words into gibberish. 3. **The Key (Cryptographic Key)** — Only your friend has a matching key. Without it, the lock is mathematically impossible to open in a reasonable time. 4. **The Sealed Envelope (Ciphertext)** — Even if a thief intercepts the diary in transit, all they see is the locked, sealed version — useless without the key. 5. **The Wax Signature (Digital Hash / HMAC)** — A unique fingerprint stamped on the envelope. If anyone tries to swap the diary with a fake, the seal breaks and your friend immediately knows it was tampered with. In a mobile app: - The **diary** = user data, passwords, API keys, session tokens - The **lock** = AES, RSA, SHA algorithms - The **key** = a secret stored inside the Android Keystore / iOS Keychain - The **envelope** = HTTPS, TLS, certificate pinning - The **wax signature** = HMAC, digital signatures, integrity checks This is essentially what every secure mobile application does — at every step where sensitive data is touched, the app acts as a "vault courier." > [!NOTE] > **Core Definitions Recap:** > - **Confidentiality** → Data is readable only by authorized parties (Encryption). > - **Integrity** → Data has not been altered in transit (Hashing/HMAC). > - **Authentication** → The identity of the sender/receiver is verified (Digital Certificates, JWT). > - **Non-repudiation** → The sender cannot deny having sent the message (Digital Signatures). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Mobile Cryptography | Metric | Standard Value | Importance | |---|---|---| | **AES Key Size** | **128, 192, or 256 bits** | Industry standard, NSA-approved | | **RSA Key Size** | **≥ 2048 bits** (preferably 4096) | Minimum secure modulus for RSA | | **SHA-256 Hash Output** | **256 bits (32 bytes)** | Most widely used secure hash | | **PBKDF2 Iterations** | **≥ 10,000** (Android default: 10000) | Slows down brute-force attacks | | **TLS Version** | **TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3** | Secure transport layer minimum | | **Salt Length (passwords)** | **≥ 16 bytes** | Prevents rainbow table attacks | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Mobile Cryptography ### 2.1.1 Symmetric Encryption (AES — Advanced Encryption Standard) **Conceptual Flow:** - The **same secret key** is used for both encryption and decryption. - Extremely **fast** — ideal for encrypting large volumes of user data (files, databases, local storage). - Examples: **AES-128, AES-256**, DES (deprecated), 3DES (deprecated). **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. Generate a strong random key of 128/256 bits using `KeyGenerator`. 2. Store this key in a **secure container** — Android Keystore (hardware-backed) on Android, Keychain on iOS. 3. When user data (e.g., a text file) needs protection, pass it through AES in **CBC** or **GCM mode** (GCM is preferred — it provides authentication). 4. Prepend the random **Initialization Vector (IV)** to the ciphertext so decryption is unique each time. 5. To decrypt, retrieve the key from Keystore, extract the IV, and reverse the cipher. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Never hardcode the symmetric key in your source code.** Anyone with the APK can decompile it and extract the key in seconds. ### 2.1.2 Asymmetric Encryption (RSA — Rivest–Shamir–Adleman) **Conceptual Flow:** - Uses a **key pair**: a `publicKey` (shared openly) and a `privateKey` (kept secret). - Anything encrypted with the public key can ONLY be decrypted with the private key. - Slower than symmetric — used for **key exchange**, **digital signatures**, and **initial authentication handshakes**. - Example: A server shares its public key; the client encrypts a randomly generated AES key with it and sends it back. Both parties now share the same symmetric key (this is the **TLS handshake** simplified). ### 2.1.3 Hashing (SHA — Secure Hash Algorithm) **Conceptual Flow:** - A **one-way function** — you cannot reverse the hash back to the original input. - Same input always produces the same output of fixed length. - Used for **password storage** (you store the hash, not the password), **file integrity verification**, and **digital signatures**. - **SHA-256** is the de-facto standard. MD5 and SHA-1 are **broken** — never use them. ### 2.1.4 Password Hashing with Salt + Key Derivation For password storage, **plain SHA-256 is NOT enough** because attackers can use **rainbow tables** (pre-computed hash dictionaries). The solution is: - **Salt**: A random string unique per user, prepended to the password before hashing. - **Key Derivation Function (KDF)**: PBKDF2, bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 — these algorithms are intentionally **slow** and **iterative**, making brute-force attacks computationally expensive. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates the essential cryptographic formulas, parameters, and best-practice constants that KTU examiners expect students to know for both theory and lab viva questions. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Parameters / Units | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **AES Key Sizes** | $K \in \{128, 192, 256\}\ \text{bits}$ | Bits | Symmetric encryption | | 2 | **RSA Modulus** | $n = p \times q$ where $p, q$ are large primes | Bits (≥ 2048) | Asymmetric encryption | | 3 | **RSA Complexity** | $\text{Security} \approx 2^{k/2}$ where $k$ is key size in bits | Exponential ops | Brute-force resistance | | 4 | **SHA-256 Output** | $H(m) = \text{256-bit digest}$ | 32 bytes | Hashing | | 5 | **Salted Hash** | $H' = \text{Hash}(\text{salt} \parallel \text{password})$ | Salt ≥ 16 bytes | Password storage | | 6 | **PBKDF2** | $\text{DK} = \text{PBKDF2}(\text{HMAC-SHA256}, P, S, c, dkLen)$ | Iterations $c \geq 10000$ | Slow password hashing | | 7 | **HMAC** | $\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H((K' \oplus opad) \parallel H((K' \oplus ipad) \parallel m))$ | Key + Message | Message authentication | | 8 | **Block Size (AES)** | 128 bits (16 bytes) | Fixed | Padding required for last block | | 9 | **IV (AES-CBC)** | Random, 16 bytes, **never reused** with the same key | Bytes | Cipher mode randomness | | 10 | **JWT Expiry** | $\text{iat} + \text{exp} \leq 15\text{ min (access token)}$ | Seconds | Session management | | 11 | **TLS Handshake** | $\text{RSA}(C \rightarrow S): \text{Enc}_{PK_S}(K_{AES})$ | Key exchange | Secure channel setup | | 12 | **OWASP M1** | Improper Credential Use → use Keystore | — | Local secret storage | | 13 | **OWASP M3** | Insecure Authentication → OAuth 2.0 + JWT | — | Server auth | | 14 | **Certificate Pinning** | $\text{Pin} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{DER}(\text{Cert}))$ | Hash | Network MITM defense | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Exam Note:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **encryption** (reversible with key) and **hashing** (irreversible). A common 3-mark question asks: *"Why should passwords be hashed, not encrypted?"* — the answer is: hashing is one-way, so even if the database is leaked, the attacker cannot recover the original password. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These cryptographic primitives are the **backbone of every production mobile app** in the industry: - **WhatsApp / Signal** — Use the **Signal Protocol** (X3DH + Double Ratchet), built on a combination of X25519 (asymmetric), AES-256 (symmetric), and HMAC-SHA256 (integrity) for end-to-end encryption of messages. - **Google Pay / Apple Pay** — Use **RSA-2048 + AES-256 + Tokenization** to protect card data and transaction authenticity. - **Banking Apps (e.g., SBI YONO, HDFC)** — Implement **certificate pinning**, **biometric-bound Keystore keys**, and **device attestation** to prevent fraud. - **Health Apps (e.g., Practo, MyFitnessPal)** — Comply with **HIPAA / DISHA** regulations by encrypting PHI (Personal Health Information) both at rest and in transit. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In a professional mobile app development team (e.g., Razorpay, PhonePe, Swiggy), the Security Engineering vertical integrates these practices into CI/CD pipelines via tools like **MobSF (Mobile Security Framework)**, **OWASP ZAP**, and **SonarQube** — automating vulnerability scans on every code commit. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Implementation: AES-256 Encryption in Android (Kotlin) Below is the **complete, production-grade** implementation that students are expected to write in the KTU lab. Every line is annotated for clarity. ### Step 1 — Generate and Store the AES Key in Android Keystore ```kotlin import android.security.keystore.KeyGenParameterSpec import android.security.keystore.KeyProperties import java.security.KeyStore import javax.crypto.KeyGenerator object SecureKeyManager { private const val ANDROID_KEYSTORE = "AndroidKeyStore" private const val KEY_ALIAS = "MyAppAesKey" fun getOrCreateAesKey(): javax.crypto.SecretKey { val keyStore = KeyStore.getInstance(ANDROID_KEYSTORE).apply { load(null) } // If key already exists, return it keyStore.getKey(KEY_ALIAS, null)?.let { return it as javax.crypto.SecretKey } // Otherwise, generate a new AES-256 key val keyGenerator = KeyGenerator.getInstance( KeyProperties.KEY_ALGORITHM_AES, ANDROID_KEYSTORE ) val spec = KeyGenParameterSpec.Builder( KEY_ALIAS, KeyProperties.PURPOSE_ENCRYPT or KeyProperties.PURPOSE_DECRYPT ) .setBlockModes(KeyProperties.BLOCK_MODE_GCM) // GCM = authenticated encryption .setEncryptionPaddings(KeyProperties.ENCRYPTION_PADDING_NONE) .setKeySize(256) // AES-256 .setUserAuthenticationRequired(false) // Set true for biometric-bound keys .build() keyGenerator.init(spec) return keyGenerator.generateKey() } } ``` **Step-by-Step Logic:** - `KeyStore.getInstance("AndroidKeyStore")` — Access the hardware-backed secure container (TEE/StrongBox on supported devices). - `getKey(alias, null)` — If the key already exists (i.e., previously generated), we reuse it to ensure the **same key encrypts and decrypts**. - `KeyGenParameterSpec` — Declares the key's **purpose** (encrypt/decrypt), **block mode** (GCM), and **key size** (256 bits). - `setEncryptionPaddings(NONE)` — GCM is a **stream-mode** cipher, so it does not need PKCS#5/PKCS#7 padding. ### Step 2 — Encrypt Plaintext Using AES-GCM ```kotlin import javax.crypto.Cipher import javax.crypto.spec.GCMParameterSpec import android.util.Base64 object AesGcmCipher { private const val GCM_TAG_LENGTH = 128 // Authentication tag in bits private const val IV_LENGTH = 12 // Recommended IV size for GCM (bytes) data class EncryptedPayload(val iv: ByteArray, val ciphertext: ByteArray) fun encrypt(plaintext: String): EncryptedPayload { val key = SecureKeyManager.getOrCreateAesKey() val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding") val iv = ByteArray(IV_LENGTH).also { java.security.SecureRandom().nextBytes(it) } cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key, GCMParameterSpec(GCM_TAG_LENGTH, iv)) val ciphertext = cipher.doFinal(plaintext.toByteArray(Charsets.UTF_8)) return EncryptedPayload(iv, ciphertext) } fun decrypt(payload: EncryptedPayload): String { val key = SecureKeyManager.getOrCreateAesKey() val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding") cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, key, GCMParameterSpec(GCM_TAG_LENGTH, payload.iv)) val plaintextBytes = cipher.doFinal(payload.ciphertext) return String(plaintextBytes, Charsets.UTF_8) } } ``` **Step-by-Step Logic:** - A **fresh, cryptographically random IV** of 12 bytes is generated for every encryption. Reusing an IV with the same key in GCM mode is **catastrophic** — it leaks the authentication key. - `GCMParameterSpec(128, iv)` — Sets the **GCM authentication tag** length to 128 bits, the maximum. This tag is appended to the ciphertext automatically and is verified during decryption. - `doFinal()` — Performs the encryption in one call; for large files, use `update()` in a loop. - The IV is **stored alongside** the ciphertext (it is not a secret), but the **key is never** persisted outside the Keystore. ### Step 3 — Hashing Passwords with Salt (PBKDF2) ```kotlin import javax.crypto.SecretKeyFactory import javax.crypto.spec.PBEKeySpec import java.security.SecureRandom object PasswordHasher { private const val ITERATIONS = 10000 // KTU-recommended minimum private const val KEY_LENGTH = 256 // bits private const val SALT_LENGTH = 16 // bytes fun hashPassword(password: CharArray): String { val salt = ByteArray(SALT_LENGTH).also { SecureRandom().nextBytes(it) } val spec = PBEKeySpec(password, salt, ITERATIONS, KEY_LENGTH) val factory = SecretKeyFactory.getInstance("PBKDF2WithHmacSHA256") val hash = factory.generateSecret(spec).encoded // Store: salt + hash (Base64 encoded together) return Base64.encodeToString(salt + hash, Base64.NO_WRAP) } fun verifyPassword(password: CharArray, stored: String): Boolean { val combined = Base64.decode(stored, Base64.NO_WRAP) val salt = combined.copyOfRange(0, SALT_LENGTH) val originalHash = combined.copyOfRange(SALT_LENGTH, combined.size) val spec = PBEKeySpec(password, salt, ITERATIONS, KEY_LENGTH) val factory = SecretKeyFactory.getInstance("PBKDF2WithHmacSHA256") val candidateHash = factory.generateSecret(spec).encoded // Constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks return java.security.MessageDigest.isEqual(originalHash, candidateHash) } } ``` **Step-by-Step Logic:** - A **random salt** of 16 bytes is generated per user — this defeats rainbow tables because two users with the same password will produce **different hashes**. - **10,000 iterations** of PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 — this makes each password guess take ~10ms, so brute-forcing a 6-character password becomes computationally prohibitive. - `MessageDigest.isEqual()` — Uses a **constant-time comparison** to prevent **timing attacks** (where an attacker measures response time to guess characters one by one). - The combined string (salt + hash, Base64-encoded) is what is stored in the database. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Implementation: RSA Asymmetric Encryption (Java/Android) ```kotlin import java.security.KeyPairGenerator import java.security.KeyPair import javax.crypto.Cipher import java.util.Base64 object RsaCipher { fun generateKeyPair(): KeyPair { val generator = KeyPairGenerator.getInstance("RSA") generator.initialize(2048) // 2048-bit key = KTU minimum return generator.generateKeyPair() } fun encrypt(plaintext: String, publicKey: java.security.PublicKey): String { val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("RSA/ECB/OAEPWithSHA-256AndMGF1Padding") cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, publicKey) val bytes = cipher.doFinal(plaintext.toByteArray()) return Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(bytes) } fun decrypt(ciphertextB64: String, privateKey: java.security.PrivateKey): String { val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("RSA/ECB/OAEPWithSHA-256AndMGF1Padding") cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, privateKey) val bytes = cipher.doFinal(Base64.getDecoder().decode(ciphertextB64)) return String(bytes) } } ``` **Step-by-Step Logic:** - **OAEPWithSHA-256AndMGF1Padding** is the **only** acceptable RSA padding in modern code. The older `RSA/ECB/PKCS1Padding` is vulnerable to **Bleichenbacher's attack**. - RSA can only encrypt data **smaller than the key size** minus padding overhead (≈ 190 bytes for 2048-bit key with OAEP). For larger payloads, RSA is used only to **encrypt a randomly generated AES key** — this is the **hybrid encryption** pattern. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection Guide | Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Reasoning | KTU-Relevant Constant | |---|---|---|---| | Encrypt local user files | **AES-256-GCM** | Fast, authenticated, hardware-accelerated | 256-bit key, 12-byte IV | | Store passwords in DB | **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256** (or Argon2) | Slow, salted, brute-force resistant | ≥ 10,000 iterations | | Verify file integrity | **SHA-256** | Standard, fast, collision-resistant | 256-bit digest | | API authentication tokens | **JWT signed with HS256 or RS256** | Stateless, scalable, signed | 15-min access, 7-day refresh | | Network transport | **TLS 1.2 / 1.3** | Industry standard, mutually authenticated | Min RSA-2048 server cert | | Mobile-to-server key exchange | **RSA-OAEP** (or X25519 ECDH) | Asymmetric, slow but secure | 2048-bit key minimum | | Authenticated messaging | **HMAC-SHA256** | Integrity + authenticity, lightweight | 256-bit MAC | | Block storage (e.g., Room DB) | **SQLCipher** (AES-256) | Transparent DB encryption | 256-bit page key | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Secure Mobile App Architecture This block diagram illustrates how all the cryptographic primitives integrate into a production mobile app's data flow. ```mermaid flowchart TB user[User Input] --> validate{Input
Validation} validate -->|Invalid| reject[Reject and Log] validate -->|Valid| authn[Authentication
Layer] authn -->|Login| oauth[OAuth 2.0 + JWT] authn -->|Biometric| bio[BiometricPrompt API] oauth --> session{Session
Established?} bio --> session session -->|Yes| appLogic[App Business Logic] session -->|No| deny[Access Denied] appLogic --> readData[Read Sensitive Data] appLogic --> writeData[Write Sensitive Data] readData --> decryptAes[AES-256-GCM
Decryption] writeData --> encryptAes[AES-256-GCM
Encryption] decryptAes --> keystore1[Android Keystore] encryptAes --> keystore1 keystore1 --> tee[Hardware TEE / StrongBox] appLogic --> network[Network Call] network --> tls[TLS 1.2/1.3
HTTPS] tls --> pin[Certificate
Pinning] pin --> server[Backend Server] appLogic --> password[Password Storage] password --> pbkdf2[PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256
10K Iterations + Salt] pbkdf2 --> db[(Encrypted Database
SQLCipher)] server --> rsa[RSA-OAEP
Key Exchange] rsa --> server classDef secure fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px classDef crypto fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007bff,stroke-width:2px classDef storage fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px class decryptAes,encryptAes,tls,pin,rsa,pbkdf2 crypto class keystore1,tee,db storage class authn,oauth,bio,validate secure ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Secure Login Flow with PBKDF2 + AES + TLS ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant U as User participant App as Mobile App participant KS as Android Keystore participant Net as TLS Channel participant Srv as Auth Server participant DB as Server DB U->>App: Enter email + password App->>App: Validate input format (regex, length) App->>App: PBKDF2(password, salt, 10K) -> hash App->>KS: Retrieve AES session key KS-->>App: Return SecretKey (AES-256) App->>App: AES-GCM encrypt(loginPayload) App->>Net: POST /login (HTTPS, TLS 1.3) Net->>Net: Certificate pinning check Net->>Srv: Forward encrypted request Srv->>DB: Look up user record DB-->>Srv: Stored salt + hash Srv->>Srv: PBKDF2(received, salt, 10K) Srv->>Srv: Constant-time compare hashes alt Hash matches Srv->>Srv: Generate JWT (HS256, exp 15min) Srv-->>Net: 200 OK + JWT Net-->>App: Encrypted JWT response App->>KS: Store JWT in EncryptedSharedPreferences App-->>U: Navigate to Home Screen else Hash mismatch Srv-->>Net: 401 Unauthorized Net-->>App: Login Failed App-->>U: Show error (generic message) end ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Hybrid Encryption Pattern (RSA + AES) This is the **canonical** pattern used by HTTPS, PGP, and end-to-end encrypted messengers. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Sender[Sender Side] A[Plaintext Message] --> B[Generate random
AES-256 key] B --> C[AES-GCM encrypt
message with key] C --> D[Ciphertext M] B --> E[RSA encrypt
key with Public Key] E --> F[Encrypted Key K] end D --> G[Transmit over network] F --> G G --> H[Receiver Side] H --> I[RSA decrypt K
with Private Key] I --> J[Recovered AES key] H --> K[AES-GCM decrypt M
with recovered key] K --> L[Original Plaintext] classDef crypto fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007bff,stroke-width:2px classDef net fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px class B,C,E,I,K crypto class G,H net ``` ## 4.4 OWASP Mobile Top 10 — Mitigation Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OWASP[OWASP Mobile Top 10 - 2024] M1[M1: Improper Credential Use] M2[M2: Inadequate Supply Chain Security] M3[M3: Insecure Authentication/Authorization] M4[M4: Insufficient Input/Output Validation] M5[M5: Insecure Communication] M6[M6: Inadequate Privacy Controls] M7[M7: Insufficient Binary Protections] M8[M8: Security Misconfiguration] M9[M9: Insecure Data Storage] M10[M10: Insufficient Cryptography] end subgraph Mitigations[Defense in Depth] KS[Android Keystore + StrongBox] SAST[SAST Tools: SonarQube, MobSF] OAUTH[OAuth 2.0 + Biometric] VAL[Input Validation + Output Encoding] TLS2[TLS 1.3 + Certificate Pinning] PRIV[Privacy by Design + Data Minimization] RASP[RASP + Code Obfuscation ProGuard/R8] CFG[Secure Defaults + Manifest Hardening] SQLC[SQLCipher + EncryptedSharedPreferences] AES2[AES-256 + RSA-2048 + SHA-256] end M1 --> KS M2 --> SAST M3 --> OAUTH M4 --> VAL M5 --> TLS2 M6 --> PRIV M7 --> RASP M8 --> CFG M9 --> SQLC M10 --> AES2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be **concise (3–5 sentences)** and **definition-driven** with one supporting example. KTU examiners typically expect the key term, the standard, and the practical implementation. --- ### Question 1: Define the OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerability "Insecure Data Storage" and list three techniques to prevent it. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]` **Model Answer:** **Insecure Data Storage (M9)** refers to the vulnerability that arises when sensitive user data (such as authentication tokens, personal information, passwords, or financial records) is stored on the device in a way that can be easily accessed by attackers, malicious apps, or via a rooted/jailbroken device. **Three prevention techniques:** 1. **Use platform-provided secure storage** — Store credentials in the **Android Keystore** (hardware-backed) or **iOS Keychain**. For shared preferences, use `EncryptedSharedPreferences` (Jetpack Security) which provides transparent AES-256 encryption. 2. **Encrypt the local database** — Use **SQLCipher** (256-bit AES) for SQLite/Room databases. This protects data even if the device is physically accessed. 3. **Avoid storing sensitive data altogether** — Apply the **principle of data minimization**. For session tokens, prefer server-side storage with short-lived JWTs. Never log sensitive information using `Log.d()` in production builds (use **ProGuard** to strip logs). **Mark Distribution (KTU Standard):** - Definition of Insecure Data Storage → **1 mark** - Three prevention techniques (½ mark each) → **1.5 marks** - Practical example or library name → **0.5 marks** --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric encryption. State one use case for each. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Symmetric Encryption (AES) | Asymmetric Encryption (RSA) | |---|---|---| | **Keys Used** | Single shared secret key | Key pair: public + private | | **Speed** | Very fast (hardware-accelerated) | Slow (factorization-based) | | **Key Distribution** | Difficult — must be shared securely | Public key is freely distributable | | **Key Size** | 128 / 256 bits | ≥ 2048 bits | | **Use Case** | Encrypting large files, local database, disk | Digital signatures, SSL/TLS handshake, key exchange | **Use Case 1 (Symmetric):** Encrypting the user's locally stored notes file using **AES-256-GCM** with the key held in the Android Keystore. **Use Case 2 (Asymmetric):** The mobile app uses the **server's RSA-2048 public key** to encrypt a randomly generated AES session key, which is then used for the rest of the conversation — the **hybrid encryption** pattern used in HTTPS. **Mark Distribution:** - 4 points of difference (½ mark each) → **2 marks** - One use case for each → **1 mark** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions offer an **internal choice** — students answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (both 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each). The choice is typically between a "code/implementation-heavy" question and a "theory/comparison-heavy" question. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): Implementation-Focused** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]` **(a)** Explain the concept of **password hashing using PBKDF2 with salt**. Write the complete Kotlin code to (i) hash a user password during registration and (ii) verify the password during login. Mention why `MessageDigest.isEqual()` is preferred over `Arrays.equals()`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design and implement a Kotlin function to perform **AES-256-GCM encryption and decryption** of a user-supplied plaintext string. Your implementation must: (i) generate the key inside the Android Keystore, (ii) use a fresh random IV for every encryption, and (iii) store the IV alongside the ciphertext. Explain the role of the **GCM authentication tag** in detecting tampering. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks** **Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):** - **PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2)** applies a **pseudo-random function** (HMAC-SHA256) iteratively to the password combined with a random salt. The iteration count (≥ 10,000) makes each guess expensive for an attacker, while the **salt** ensures that two users with identical passwords produce different hashes (defeats rainbow tables). - The output is a fixed-length derived key (e.g., 256 bits) that is stored in the database — never the original password. **Code (Registration) — 2 marks:** ```kotlin fun registerUser(email: String, password: CharArray): UserRecord { val salt = ByteArray(16).also { SecureRandom().nextBytes(it) } val spec = PBEKeySpec(password, salt, 10000, 256) val hash = SecretKeyFactory.getInstance("PBKDF2WithHmacSHA256") .generateSecret(spec).encoded return UserRecord(email, Base64.encodeToString(salt + hash, Base64.NO_WRAP)) } ``` **Code (Login Verification) — 1 mark:** ```kotlin fun verify(password: CharArray, stored: String): Boolean { val combined = Base64.decode(stored, Base64.NO_WRAP) val salt = combined.copyOfRange(0, 16) val expected = combined.copyOfRange(16, combined.size) val spec = PBEKeySpec(password, salt, 10000, 256) val actual = SecretKeyFactory.getInstance("PBKDF2WithHmacSHA256") .generateSecret(spec).encoded return MessageDigest.isEqual(expected, actual) } ``` **Why `MessageDigest.isEqual()` — 1 mark:** - `Arrays.equals()` is a **short-circuit** comparison: it returns `false` as soon as the first mismatched byte is found. An attacker can **measure the response time** to deduce the position of the first wrong character, leaking password information byte by byte. - `MessageDigest.isEqual()` uses a **constant-time comparison** — it always checks all bytes regardless of where the mismatch occurs, making **timing attacks infeasible**. #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks** **Architecture Explanation (2 marks):** - **Android Keystore** is a hardware-backed container (TEE — Trusted Execution Environment) that stores cryptographic keys such that the raw key material **never leaves the secure hardware** — even if the OS is compromised, the key cannot be extracted. - For every encryption call, a **new 12-byte IV** is generated using `SecureRandom`. This IV is **not secret** and is stored alongside the ciphertext so the same key can reconstruct the plaintext later. **Code — 5 marks (full implementation):** ```kotlin data class CipherResult(val iv: ByteArray, val ciphertext: ByteArray) fun encrypt(plain: String): CipherResult { val key = SecretKey getOrCreate from Keystore // (Step 1) val iv = ByteArray(12).also { SecureRandom().nextBytes(it) } val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding") cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key, GCMParameterSpec(128, iv)) val ct = cipher.doFinal(plain.toByteArray(Charsets.UTF_8)) return CipherResult(iv, ct) // Store both in DB } fun decrypt(result: CipherResult): String { val key = SecretKey getOrCreate from Keystore val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding") cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, key, GCMParameterSpec(128, result.iv)) return String(cipher.doFinal(result.ciphertext), Charsets.UTF_8) } ``` **Role of GCM Authentication Tag — bonus credit:** - GCM appends a **128-bit authentication tag** computed over both the ciphertext and the IV. During decryption, if even **one bit** of the ciphertext is modified, the tag verification fails and a `AEADBadTagException` is thrown. This provides **confidentiality + integrity in one step**, unlike AES-CBC which requires a separate HMAC. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Theory-Focused (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set B]` **(a)** Discuss the **OWASP Mobile Top 10** vulnerabilities. For any **five** of them, explain the attack scenario and provide the **mitigation technique** with an Android-specific implementation detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **Certificate Pinning**? Explain why it is necessary even when using HTTPS/TLS. With a code example, demonstrate how to implement certificate pinning in Android using **OkHttp's CertificatePinner**. Also discuss its **drawbacks and operational challenges**. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks** **Introduction (1 mark):** The **OWASP Mobile Top 10 (2024)** is a curated list of the most critical security risks faced by mobile applications, maintained by the Open Web Application Security Project. **Five Vulnerabilities (each 1 mark = 5 marks; brief explanation + Android fix):** 1. **M1 – Improper Credential Use** - **Attack:** App hardcodes API keys or stores passwords in plain `SharedPreferences`. - **Fix:** Use **Android Keystore** for cryptographic keys and **`EncryptedSharedPreferences`** (from `androidx.security:security-crypto`) for storing sensitive values. 2. **M3 – Insecure Authentication / Authorization** - **Attack:** Client-side trust — the app believes the user is admin because a local flag is set. - **Fix:** Validate every request **server-side** using **OAuth 2.0 + JWT** with short expiry (15 min) and refresh-token rotation. Use **BiometricPrompt API** for step-up authentication. 3. **M4 – Insufficient Input/Output Validation** - **Attack:** SQL injection via unsanitized form fields into a Room/SQLite query. - **Fix:** Use **parameterized queries** (Room's `@Query` with `:param` placeholders) and **regex-based validators** at the UI layer. 4. **M5 – Insecure Communication** - **Attack:** MITM (Man-in-the-Middle) using a self-signed certificate installed by the user. - **Fix:** Enforce **TLS 1.2/1.3** in `networkSecurityConfig.xml`, set `cleartextTrafficPermitted="false"`, and use **Certificate Pinning** with OkHttp. 5. **M9 – Insecure Data Storage** - **Attack:** An attacker with physical access (or via ADB on a debug build) reads the app's `/data/data/` directory. - **Fix:** Use **SQLCipher** for the database and **EncryptedFile** for any file I/O. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern mobile security requires a **defense-in-depth** strategy — no single control is sufficient. The OWASP Mobile Top 10 provides a prioritized checklist that aligns with industry practices and regulatory requirements (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS). #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks** **Definition (1 mark):** **Certificate Pinning** is a security mechanism in which the mobile app is hardcoded to **trust only a specific set of SSL/TLS server certificates** (identified by their public key hash, typically SHA-256) and reject all others, even if they are signed by a trusted CA. **Why Necessary (1.5 marks):** - The default TLS trust store on Android includes **~150+ root CAs**. If **any one** of these is compromised (as happened with DigiNotar in 2011 and Symantec in 2018), an attacker can issue a valid certificate for any domain and perform a **MITM attack**. - Pinning narrows the trust to **only the certificates you control** — the chain of trust shrinks from "any of 150 CAs" to "exactly 1 expected public key hash." **Code Implementation (3 marks):** ```kotlin val client = OkHttpClient.Builder() .certificatePinner( CertificatePinner.Builder() .add( "api.mybank.com", "sha256/AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA=" // Public key hash ) .add( "api.mybank.com", "sha256/BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB=" // Backup key ) .build() ) .build() ``` The `sha256/` prefix is mandatory; OkHttp computes the hash of the certificate's **SubjectPublicKeyInfo (SPKI)**. **Drawbacks / Operational Challenges (1.5 marks):** - **Key Rotation Risk:** If the server's certificate expires or is rotated (which happens every 90 days with Let's Encrypt), the app must be updated and re-released. Failure to do so causes the entire app fleet to be **locked out**. - **Maintenance Overhead:** Large enterprises maintain a **pin set** with at least two pins (primary + backup) to allow for rotation without app updates. - **Debug Complexity:** Pinning makes it impossible to inspect traffic with tools like Charles Proxy or mitmproxy during development unless the developer pin is added. - **CDN Limitations:** When using CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai), the certificate is shared across many customers — pinning is generally avoided in favor of **SPKI pinning** at the CA level. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4 (Lab) Examinations:** > > 1. **Confusing hashing with encryption** — Saying "passwords are encrypted with SHA-256" is **wrong**. SHA-256 is a hash, not encryption. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this fundamental error. > > 2. **Storing the symmetric key in code** — If a student writes `val key = "MySecretKey123".toByteArray()` instead of using the Keystore, the examiner will **mark the security part as 0** because the entire purpose of using the Keystore is defeated. > > 3. **Reusing the IV in AES-GCM** — The IV MUST be freshly generated for every encryption. Reusing an IV with the same key in GCM mode is **catastrophic** and will result in a 0 for the implementation mark. > > 4. **Using MD5 or SHA-1** — Both are **cryptographically broken**. Using them for password storage or integrity verification is a serious security flaw; KTU examiners deduct full marks for this. > > 5. **Skipping salt explanation** — When explaining password hashing, students often forget to mention **why** salt is needed (rainbow table defense). This loses 1 mark in a 7-mark question. > > 6. **Missing input validation** — In any lab program that accepts user input (e.g., login form, registration form), failing to demonstrate **regex validation** or **length checks** results in a deduction under M4 (OWASP). > > 7. **Hardcoding API keys in BuildConfig** — While `BuildConfig.API_KEY` is a common pattern, KTU's advanced questions expect students to mention that even `BuildConfig` values can be extracted from APKs via tools like **APKTool** or **jadx** — true security requires **runtime fetching** or **server-side proxying**. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Facts for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 4):** - ✅ **Three pillars of security** — Confidentiality (encryption), Integrity (hashing/HMAC), Authentication (digital signatures, certificates). - ✅ **AES-256-GCM** is the recommended symmetric mode — provides both encryption and integrity in one operation. - ✅ **Key sizes:** AES-128/256, RSA ≥ 2048, SHA-256 output = 256 bits. - ✅ **Android Keystore** is the ONLY acceptable location for storing cryptographic keys in production Android apps. - ✅ **PBKDF2 with salt + ≥ 10,000 iterations** is the minimum for password storage; Argon2 is preferred for new projects. - ✅ **Never reuse an IV** in AES-GCM mode with the same key — it leaks the authentication key. - ✅ **Use `MessageDigest.isEqual()`** for hash/password comparison — constant-time, timing-attack-safe. - ✅ **TLS 1.2 / 1.3** is mandatory for all network calls; disable cleartext traffic in `networkSecurityConfig.xml`. - ✅ **Certificate Pinning** defends against compromised CAs; always pin **two keys** (primary + backup) for rotation. - ✅ **Hashing is one-way; encryption is two-way** — passwords are **hashed**, not encrypted. - ✅ **OWASP M9 (Insecure Data Storage)** is mitigated by SQLCipher + EncryptedSharedPreferences + Keystore. - ✅ **OAEP padding is mandatory for RSA** — never use `PKCS1Padding` in modern code. - ✅ **MD5 and SHA-1 are broken** — never use them in any security-sensitive context. - ✅ **Hybrid encryption** (RSA + AES) is the standard for encrypting data larger than the RSA key size. - ✅ **BiometricPrompt API** is the modern way to bind a Keystore key to user biometrics (StrongBox if available). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question in this module, structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Working mechanism with diagram/algorithm → (3) Android-specific code/imports → (4) OWASP/Industry mapping → (5) Trade-offs / Drawbacks**. This 5-part structure aligns perfectly with the KTU 2024 marking scheme and consistently scores **12+ marks** out of 14.

topics.title: Lab: Developing and Deploying a Cross-Platform App Using Flutter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT744 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lab: Developing and Deploying a Cross-Platform App Using Flutter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flutter** is an open-source UI software development kit (SDK) created by Google, used to build natively compiled applications for mobile (Android, iOS), web, and desktop from a **single codebase**. It uses the **Dart** programming language and follows a reactive, widget-based architecture where the entire UI is constructed as a hierarchical tree of immutable widgets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Perspective):**
> *Cross-platform app development using Flutter* refers to the engineering practice of writing **one unified source code** that compiles to **platform-specific native binaries** (ARM/x64 machine code) for multiple target operating systems using Flutter's layered rendering engine (Skia/Impeller).

For the PEITT744 (Mobile App Development) lab, the **official KTU 2024 lab objective** states:

> *"To design, develop, test, and deploy a cross-platform mobile application using the Flutter framework, demonstrating widget composition, navigation, state management, and release build generation."*

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition | KTU Board Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **SDK** | Software Development Kit | Bundles compiler, libraries, tools |
| **Dart** | Compiled, object-oriented language | Flutter's only supported language |
| **Widget** | Immutable UI description block | Fundamental building unit |
| **Hot Reload** | Sub-second code injection | Boosts development speed by **~3x** |
| **AOT** | Ahead-Of-Time compilation | Used in release builds |
| **JIT** | Just-In-Time compilation | Used in debug/dev mode |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Lego Set"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Think of Flutter as a **"Universal Lego Set"**. Imagine a child in Kerala wants to build a toy car, a robot, AND a plane. Instead of buying three different kits, they buy **one universal kit** (Flutter SDK) where the same Lego blocks (Widgets) can be snapped together to build any model. The blocks automatically take the right shape depending on whether the child puts them on a "car base" (Android), a "plane base" (iOS), or a "web base" (Chrome).

### Why This Matters for KTU Exams
Flutter is **not** a webview wrapper (like Cordova/Ionic) and **not** a JavaScript bridge (like React Native). It **sketches pixels directly** on a canvas using the **Skia/Impeller rendering engine**, achieving **60–120 FPS** consistent performance — a frequent viva question.

## 1.3 Physical / System Constants (Bold as Required)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Default Dart entry point:** `lib/main.dart`
> - **Standard Flutter SDK stable version (2024):** `3.24.x` (LTS-aligned)
> - **Minimum Android API Level:** `21` (Android 5.0 Lollipop)
> - **Minimum iOS deployment target:** `iOS 12.0`
> - **Default Material Design version:** `Material 3 (Material You)`
> - **Dart null safety:** **Enabled by default** since Flutter 2.0
> - **Hot Reload latency:** **< 300 ms** on a standard laptop

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flutter's Three-Layer Architecture (Theoretical Foundation)

Flutter is built on **three distinct architectural layers**, each critical for exam answers:

### Layer 1 — The Embedder (Platform Glue)
- **Role:** Acts as the host process.
- **Contains:** Android (Java/Kotlin) and iOS (Objective-C/Swift) shells.
- **Function:** Forwards OS events (touch, sensor, lifecycle) to the Flutter engine.

### Layer 2 — The Engine (C/C++ Core)
- **Role:** Renders the UI.
- **Components:**
  * **Skia** (legacy) / **Impeller** (modern, default in Flutter 3.10+)
  * **Dart runtime** + **Garbage Collector** (generational, parallel)
  * **Animation ticker** and **text shaping**

### Layer 3 — The Framework (Dart Layer)
- **Role:** Where the developer writes code.
- **Components:**
  * `material.dart`, `cupertino.dart`, `widgets.dart`
  * Animation, painting, gesture, foundation libraries

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** "**E**mb**E**dder hosts **E**ngine, Engine powers **F**ramework" → **EEEF**.

## 2.2 The Widget Tree (Operational Core)

Every Flutter app is a **tree of widgets**. There are **two super-classes**:

### A. StatelessWidget
- **Immutable** once built.
- No internal mutable state.
- `build()` runs **once** (or when parent rebuilds).
- **Analogy:** A printed photograph — you can't change what's on it.

### B. StatefulWidget
- Has a mutable **State object**.
- `setState(() { ... })` triggers a rebuild.
- **Analogy:** A digital whiteboard — you can erase and rewrite.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Calling `setState()` inside a StatelessWidget. The compiler will throw `Error: setState() called on a non-State object`. Always check `extends StatelessWidget` vs `extends StatefulWidget` first.

## 2.3 Lifecycle Methods (High-Yield for Part A)

For a `StatefulWidget`, the **lifecycle order** in KTU diagrams is:

$$
\text{createState} \rightarrow \text{mounted} \rightarrow \text{initState} \rightarrow \text{didChangeDependencies} \rightarrow \text{build} \rightarrow \text{didUpdateWidget} \rightarrow \text{deactivate} \rightarrow \text{dispose}
$$

| Method | When Called | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| `initState()` | Once, before first build | Initialize controllers, fetch data |
| `didChangeDependencies()` | When inherited widget changes | Theme/locale reactivity |
| `build()` | On every rebuild | Return the widget tree |
| `dispose()` | When widget is removed | Cancel timers, close streams |

## 2.4 Build Modes (Debug vs Release)

| Aspect | **Debug Mode** | **Release Mode** |
|---|---|---|
| Compilation | **JIT** (Just-in-Time) | **AOT** (Ahead-of-Time) |
| Hot Reload | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Performance | Slow (instrumented) | **Native ARM speed** |
| Binary Size | Large (debug symbols) | **Optimized, smaller** |
| Command | `flutter run` | `flutter run --release` |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RENDERING NOTE:** All vertical pipe `\|` symbols use `\mid` for LaTeX/Markdown compatibility.

| Symbol/Concept | Expression / Value | KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| Hot Reload latency | $T_{\text{reload}} < 300 \text{ ms}$ | Lab viva |
| Frame rate target | $f_{\text{fps}} \in \left\{ 60,\, 120 \right\}$ Hz | Performance |
| Widget tree depth cost | $O(n)$ layout passes | Optimization |
| Pubspec dependencies | `dependencies:` block | Lab exam |
| Dart version | $\geq 3.0$ (2024 stable) | Environment check |
| Min Android SDK | $\text{API} \geq 21$ | Manifest |
| Material Design | $\text{Material 3 (v1.x)}$ | UI Standards |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Startup Time (cold launch):** Flutter apps average **~1.2s** vs React Native's **~2.5s**.
- **Adopted by:** Google Pay, BMW, Toyota, Alibaba, eBay, Nubank.
- **In KTU context:** Industry-ready framework for final-year projects and B.Tech portfolios.

## 3.4 Phase 4 — Pub-Spec & Dependency Management

The `pubspec.yaml` file is the project manifest:

```yaml
name: ktu_campus_app
description: A KTU 2024 cross-platform app
publish_to: 'none'
version: 1.0.0+1

environment:
  sdk: '>=3.0.0 <4.0.0'
  flutter: '>=3.24.0'

dependencies:
  flutter:
    sdk: flutter
  cupertino_icons: ^1.0.6

dev_dependencies:
  flutter_test:
    sdk: flutter
  flutter_lints: ^4.0.0

flutter:
  uses-material-design: true
```

**Analytical Reasoning for Each Block:**

- **`environment:`** — Enforces Dart and Flutter versions. **Locks reproducibility.**
- **`dependencies:`** — Runtime libraries (e.g., `http`, `provider`).
- **`dev_dependencies:`** — Test/lint tools, NOT shipped in release.
- **`uses-material-design: true`** — Bundles Material icon fonts.

## 3.5 Phase 5 — Build & Deployment (Analytical Sequence)

### Step 8: Run on Emulator / Physical Device
```bash

$ flutter run                  # Deploys in debug mode
```

### Step 9: Build the Release APK (Android)
```bash
$ flutter build apk --release
# Output: build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk
```

### Step 10: Build the App Bundle (Play Store format)
```bash
$ flutter build appbundle --release
# Output: build/app/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **APK vs AAB:** KTU theory questions often ask the difference. **APK** is a single installable file. **AAB (Android App Bundle)** is a dynamic delivery format used by the Google Play Store to generate optimized APKs per device.

### Step 11: Deployment Checklist
1. Generate signing key: `keytool -genkey -v -keystore ~/ktu-key.jks -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -validity 10000 -alias ktu`

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Flutter and list any two advantages of using it for cross-platform mobile app development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Flutter* is an open-source UI SDK developed by **Google** that uses the **Dart** programming language to build natively compiled applications for Android, iOS, web, and desktop from a **single codebase**.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Single Codebase, Multiple Platforms:** The same Dart code compiles to native ARM code for Android, iOS, and JavaScript for web — drastically reducing development time and cost.
2. **Hot Reload:** Sub-second code injection allows developers to see UI changes in **< 300 ms**, accelerating the iteration cycle by **~3x** compared to native development.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **NOT** write "Flutter is a framework like React Native." That earns **0 marks** for the definition. Flutter is an **SDK**, not a framework — a frequent viva trap.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between `StatelessWidget` and `StatefulWidget` in Flutter. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `StatelessWidget` | `StatefulWidget` |
|---|---|---|
| Mutability | **Immutable** | Has mutable `State` object |
| Rebuild trigger | Parent rebuilds | `setState()` call |
| Lifecycle | `build()` once | `initState`, `build`, `dispose` |
| Memory footprint | Lower | Slightly higher |

- **StatelessWidget example:** A `LogoScreen` displaying a static image and the text "Made with Flutter."
- **StatefulWidget example:** A `LoginScreen` with a `TextEditingController` whose internal value changes on user input.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B Module 4 has **internal choice**. Both options below carry equal marks. Answer **only one** in the exam.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, S8 PEITT744]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer architecture of Flutter** — Framework, Engine, and Embedder. Discuss how each layer contributes to cross-platform execution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Flutter program to build a **multi-screen app** with a HomeScreen and a DetailsScreen. The HomeScreen should display a counter that increments on tapping a `FloatingActionButton`, and an `ElevatedButton` to navigate to the DetailsScreen. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Three-Layer Architecture of Flutter (Block Diagram):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|           FRAMEWORK LAYER (Dart)                 |
|  - Material & Cupertino Widgets                  |
|  - Animation, Painting, Gestures                 |
+--------------------------------------------------+
                     |
                     v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|           ENGINE LAYER (C/C++)                   |
|  - Skia / Impeller Graphics Engine               |
|  - Dart Runtime (JIT / AOT)                      |
|  - Text Layout & Composition                     |
+--------------------------------------------------+
                     |
                     v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|           EMBEDDER LAYER (Platform)              |
|  - Android (Kotlin/Java)                         |
|  - iOS (Objective-C/Swift)                       |
|  - Web (JavaScript)                              |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Framework Layer (Dart):** The topmost layer where developers write application code. It includes the widget catalog (Material, Cupertino), animation APIs, and rendering primitives. All app-level logic resides here. **(2 marks)**

2. **Engine Layer (C/C++):** Written primarily in C++, this layer is the **heart of Flutter**. It contains:
   - **Skia/Impeller:** A 2D graphics engine that draws widgets directly onto a canvas — bypassing the platform's native UI components.
   - **Dart Runtime:** Supports both **JIT (debug)** and **AOT (release)** compilation.
   - **Text Shaper:** Handles complex text rendering across languages.
   This ensures **identical UI** across platforms. **(2.5 marks)**

3. **Embedder Layer:** Platform-specific code that hosts the Flutter engine. It forwards OS events (touch, sensor, lifecycle) to the engine and presents the rendered output on screen. Different embedders exist for Android, iOS, Windows, macOS, Linux, and Web. **(2.5 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the layers (top-to-bottom):** **F**ramework → **E**ngine → **E**mbedder → **"FEE"**.

---
