### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Flutter widget lifecycle** for a `StatefulWidget`. List the major lifecycle methods in order and state what each is typically used for. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **process of building and deploying a Flutter app to the Google Play Store**, including APK vs AAB distinction, signing key generation, and the `flutter build` commands used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Flutter StatefulWidget Lifecycle (in order):**

1. **`createState()`** — Called by the framework when the widget is inserted into the tree. Returns an instance of the `State` class. **(0.5 marks)**

2. **`mounted` becomes `true`** — Internal flag set by the framework. **(0.5 marks)**

3. **`initState()`** — Called **once**, after `createState` and before the first `build()`. Ideal for initializing controllers, subscribing to streams, or kicking off network calls. **Cannot** call `BuildContext` dependencies here. **(1 mark)**

4. **`didChangeDependencies()`** — Called after `initState` and whenever an `InheritedWidget` that this state depends on changes. Use it for theme/locale reactivity. **(1 mark)**

5. **`build()`** — Called frequently. Must return a `Widget` describing the UI. **Keep this method pure** — no side effects. **(1.5 marks)**

6. **`didUpdateWidget(oldWidget)`** — Called when the parent widget rebuilds and passes a new configuration. Use it to compare old vs new props. **(1 mark)**

7. **`deactivate()`** — Called when the widget is removed from the tree but **might be re-inserted** later (during transitions). **(0.5 marks)**

8. **`dispose()`** — Called **permanently** when the widget is removed. **Cancel timers, close streams, dispose controllers** to avoid memory leaks. **(1 mark)**

**Visual Order:**

$$
\text{createState} \to \text{initState} \to \text{didChangeDeps} \to \text{build} \to \text{didUpdateWidget} \to \text{deactivate} \to \text{dispose}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Building & Deploying a Flutter App — Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Code Finalization:** Ensure all features are implemented and tested using `flutter test`. **(1 mark)**

2. **Versioning:** Update `pubspec.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   version: 1.0.0+1
   # Format: name+buildNumber
   ```
   **(0.5 marks)**

3. **Generate a Signing Key (Keystore):**
   ```bash
   $ keytool -genkey -v -keystore ~/ktu-upload.jks \
        -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -validity 10000 \
        -alias ktu-upload
   ```
   This creates a `*.jks` file — **never commit this to Git.** **(1 mark)**

4. **Create `android/key.properties`:**
   ```properties
   storePassword=yourPassword
   keyPassword=yourPassword
   keyAlias=ktu-upload
   storeFile=/home/user/ktu-upload.jks
   ```
   **(0.5 marks)**

5. **Configure `android/app/build.gradle`:**
   ```gradle
   signingConfigs {
       release {
           keyAlias keystoreProperties['keyAlias']
           keyPassword keystoreProperties['keyPassword']
           storeFile keystoreProperties['storeFile']
           storePassword keystoreProperties['storePassword']
       }
   }
   buildTypes {
       release {
           signingConfig signingConfigs.release
       }
   }
   ```
   **(1.5 marks)**

6. **Build the AAB (Play Store format):**
   ```bash
   $ flutter build appbundle --release
   # Output: build/app/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab
   ```
   **(1 mark)**

7. **Upload to Google Play Console:**
   - Sign in to [Google Play Console](https://play.google.com/console).
   - Create a new app entry → fill in store listing → upload the `.aab` file.
   - Submit for review. **(1 mark)**

8. **APK vs AAB Distinction:**

| Format | Extension | Use Case | Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **APK** | `.apk` | Direct install, sideloading | Larger, generic |
| **AAB** | `.aab` | Google Play Store upload | Smaller, device-optimized |

   **(0.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting `useMaterial3: true`** in `ThemeData` — loses 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Calling `setState()` outside `_incrementCounter`** (e.g., directly in `build()`) — throws runtime exception.
> 3. **Missing `super.key`** in constructors — Dart analyzer warning, may lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Using `==` instead of `Navigator.push`** for screen changes — completely wrong; loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not running `flutter doctor`** before submission — if any `[✗]` exists, the lab exam may be invalidated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before the exam. 5 minutes of review = guaranteed marks.**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- [x] **Flutter** = Google's open-source **UI SDK** for cross-platform apps.
- [x] **Dart** = the only language used in Flutter (object-oriented, null-safe, JIT + AOT).
- [x] **Widget** = immutable UI blueprint; everything in Flutter is a widget.
- [x] **Hot Reload** = injects updated code in **< 300 ms**; preserves app state.
- [x] **Hot Restart** = restarts the app from scratch; **destroys** state.

### 🏗️ Architecture
- [x] **3 Layers:** Framework (Dart) → Engine (C/C++ with Skia/Impeller) → Embedder (Platform).
- [x] Flutter does **NOT** use WebViews or JS bridges — it draws pixels directly.

### 🧩 Widget Rules
- [x] `StatelessWidget` → no mutable state; `build()` runs **once** or when parent rebuilds.
- [x] `StatefulWidget` → has a `State` object; call `setState()` to trigger rebuild.
- [x] `MaterialApp` is **always** the root widget for Android-styled apps.
- [x] `Scaffold` provides `AppBar`, `body`, `floatingActionButton`, `drawer`.

### 🔄 Lifecycle (in order)
- [x] `createState` → `initState` → `didChangeDependencies` → `build` → `didUpdateWidget` → `deactivate` → `dispose`.

### 🧭 Navigation
- [x] `Navigator.push(context, MaterialPageRoute(builder: ...))` — go forward.
- [x] `Navigator.pop(context)` — go back.
- [x] `Navigator.pushReplacement(...)` — replace current screen.

### 📦 Build Commands (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [x] `flutter doctor` — environment check.
- [x] `flutter create <name>` — new project.
- [x] `flutter run` — debug mode run.
- [x] `flutter run --release` — release mode run.
- [x] `flutter build apk --release` — Android APK.
- [x] `flutter build appbundle --release` — Google Play AAB.
- [x] `flutter pub get` — fetch dependencies from `pubspec.yaml`.

### 📁 Project Structure
- [x] `lib/main.dart` — entry point.
- [x] `pubspec.yaml` — manifest with dependencies.
- [x] `android/` and `ios/` — platform-specific code (do NOT modify unless necessary).
- [x] `test/` — automated tests.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- [x] Don't forget `const` constructors — performance penalty.
- [x] Don't mutate state outside `setState()`.
- [x] Don't use mutable global variables for state.
- [x] Always run `flutter doctor` first.
- [x] Always handle `dispose()` for controllers/timers.
- [x] Never commit `*.jks` keystore files to Git.

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Viva Favorites
- [x] **Why Flutter instead of React Native?** → Better performance (no JS bridge), custom rendering engine.
- [x] **JIT vs AOT?** → JIT = debug, fast iteration. AOT = release, native performance.
- [x] **APK vs AAB?** → APK = single file install; AAB = dynamic delivery for Play Store.
- [x] **Material 2 vs Material 3?** → Material 3 uses dynamic color via `colorSchemeSeed`.
- [x] **Why Dart?** → Single language for UI, business logic, and native FFI; null-safe; fast GC.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Section 5 — KTU 2024 Lab Module 4 Notes.**
> *Best of luck with your Mobile App Development lab examination!* 🚀
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lab: Preparing and Submitting Apps to the Google Play Store and Apple App Store
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT744 - MOBILE APP DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lab: Preparing and Submitting Apps to the Google Play Store and Apple App Store

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **App Store Submission (KTU 2024 Definition):** The end-to-end engineering process of preparing a production-grade mobile application — including code signing, asset generation, manifest configuration, store listing creation, compliance review, and binary upload — to make the application publicly downloadable via the **Google Play Console** (for Android) or **Apple App Store Connect** (for iOS).

In KTU lab parlance, this lab session bridges the gap between *writing code* and *shipping a product*. It validates **Course Outcomes (CO4–CO5)** of the 2024 scheme, namely:

- **CO4:** Apply release-engineering practices such as signing, versioning, and obfuscation.
- **CO5:** Demonstrate the complete deployment workflow on at least one commercial app marketplace.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Restaurant Opening" Metaphor**
> Think of releasing an app like opening a restaurant franchise:
> - The **app binary (AAB/IPA)** is the *cooking recipe + finished dish*.
> - **Code signing** is the *health & safety certificate* proving the dish is authentic and untampered.
> - The **Play Console / App Store Connect** is the *franchise headquarters* that decides if your dish is fit for the public menu.
> - The **store listing (screenshots, description, icon)** is the *menu card & window display* that attracts customers.
> - The **review process** is the *quality inspector* tasting the food before it goes public.
> Just as a chef cannot sell food without a license, a developer cannot distribute an app without proper signing, metadata, and platform approval.

### Key Entities & Standard Metrics

| Term | Platform | Standard Value |
|------|----------|----------------|
| Developer Account Fee | Google Play | **$25 USD (one-time, lifetime)** |
| Developer Program Fee | Apple | **$99 USD (per year)** |
| App Bundle Format | Google Play | **.aab (Android App Bundle)** |
| App Bundle Format | Apple | **.ipa (iOS App Archive)** |
| Min. Review Time | Google Play | **A few hours to 7 days** |
| Min. Review Time | Apple | **24 hours to 7+ days** |
| Mandatory Screenshots | Both | **At least 2 (Phone), recommended 8** |
| Min. App Icon | Both | **512 × 512 px (Play), 1024 × 1024 px (App Store)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The App Submission Pipeline (Universal Stages)

Every app submission — regardless of platform — passes through these **5 universal stages**:

1. **Pre-Submission Engineering** → signing keys, ProGuard/R8, release variants, manifest hardening.
2. **Asset & Metadata Production** → icons, screenshots, descriptions, privacy policy URL.
3. **Store Listing Configuration** → content rating, target audience, category, pricing.
4. **Binary Upload & Review** → AAB/IPA upload, automated + manual review.
5. **Release & Post-Launch** → staged rollout, crash monitoring, version updates.

### 2.2 Platform-Specific Theoretical Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Google Play uses a 3-tier signing model** (upload key, app signing key, signing key) introduced in **2021** as mandatory. **Apple uses a flat 2-tier model** (development certificate + distribution provisioning profile).

#### A. Google Play Signing Architecture
- **Upload Key:** The key the developer signs the AAB with on their machine.
- **App Signing Key:** Held securely by Google; used to sign the final APKs distributed to devices.
- **Signing Key:** Aliases for the actual APKs Google produces (per device config).

#### B. Apple Code Signing Architecture
- **Certificate Signing Request (CSR):** Generated in Keychain Access.
- **Distribution Certificate (.p12):** Issued by Apple, valid for 1 year.
- **Provisioning Profile:** Links App ID + Certificate + Devices/Entitlements.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Concept | Google Play | Apple App Store |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| Developer Account | Play Console (one-time $25) | Apple Developer Program ($99/yr) |
| Build Format | AAB / APK | IPA via Xcode Archive |
| Signing | App Signing by Google (recommended) | Distribution Certificate + Profile |
| Min. Target API | **API 34 (Android 14)** as of 2024 | **iOS 12+** (typical floor) |
| Min. SDK | Declared in `build.gradle` | Declared in Xcode Deployment Target |
| Testing Tracks | Internal, Closed, Open | TestFlight (Internal + External) |
| Review Type | Automated + Spot manual | Strict human + automated |
| Versioning | `versionCode` (int), `versionName` (string) | `CFBundleVersion` (int), `CFBundleShortVersionString` |
| Privacy Manifest | Required (Data Safety form) | **Privacy Manifest (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`)** required from **May 2024** |
| Rollout Strategy | Staged rollout (1% → 100%) | Phased release (7-day) optional |
| Update Cadence | Flexible | Same review process |
| Rejection Grounds | Policy, malware, metadata | Guideline 4 (design), 5.1.1 (privacy) |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Mastering app submission is **not optional** in the KTU 2024 scheme — it directly maps to industry roles such as **DevOps Engineer (Mobile)**, **Release Manager**, and **Mobile QA**. Companies like Infosys, TCS, and Cognizant have dedicated *Mobile Release Engineering* teams responsible for exactly these workflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 GOOGLE PLAY STORE — Complete Submission Procedure

#### Step 1: Create a Google Play Developer Account
- Navigate to **https://play.google.com/console**.
- Pay the **$25 USD** one-time registration fee via Google Payments.
- Complete identity verification (D-U-N-S number for organizations, or personal ID for individuals).
- Account activation typically takes **48 hours**.

#### Step 2: Configure App Signing
- In Play Console → **Setup → App signing**.
- Generate an upload key locally using:
  ```bash
  keytool -genkey -v -keystore my-upload-key.jks \
   -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -validity 10000 -alias upload
  ```
- Reference this keystore in `android/app/build.gradle`:
  ```gradle
  signingConfigs {
      release {
          storeFile file("my-upload-key.jks")
          storePassword System.getenv("KEYSTORE_PASSWORD")
          keyAlias "upload"
          keyPassword System.getenv("KEY_PASSWORD")
      }
  }
  ```

#### Step 3: Build a Release AAB
- In Android Studio: **Build → Generate Signed Bundle / APK**.
- Select **Android App Bundle (.aab)**.
- Choose the `release` build variant.
- Enable **minifyEnabled true** (R8/ProGuard) and **shrinkResources true** for size optimization.

#### Step 4: Prepare Store Listing Assets
- **App icon:** 512 × 512 px PNG (32-bit, no transparency).
- **Feature graphic:** 1024 × 500 px PNG/JPG.
- **Screenshots:** Minimum **2 phone screenshots**, recommended **8**; max 8 per device type.
- **Short description:** max **80 characters**.
- **Full description:** max **4000 characters**.

#### Step 5: Complete Console Forms
- **Content Rating:** Fill IARC questionnaire.
- **Target Audience & Content:** Declare if app targets children (COPPA compliance).
- **Data Safety:** Declare data collection, sharing, security practices.
- **Pricing & Distribution:** Choose Free/Paid, countries, and date.

#### Step 6: Upload AAB and Roll Out
- Play Console → **Release → Production → Create new release**.
- Upload the `.aab` file.
- Add release notes.
- Click **Review release → Start rollout to Production**.

### 3.2 APPLE APP STORE — Complete Submission Procedure

#### Step 1: Enroll in Apple Developer Program
- Visit **https://developer.apple.com/programs/enroll/**.
- Pay **$99 USD/year** (requires Apple ID, legal entity verification for organizations).

#### Step 2: Register App Identifiers & Devices
- In **Apple Developer Portal → Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles**:
  - Create an **App ID** (reverse-DNS, e.g., `com.ktu.studentapp`).
  - Register testing devices (UDID) for ad-hoc/TestFlight external testing.

#### Step 3: Generate Certificates & Profiles
- Create **Distribution Certificate** (Apple Distribution).
- Create a **Provisioning Profile** linked to the App ID and certificate.
- Download and install both in Xcode.

#### Step 4: Configure Xcode Project
- Set **Deployment Target** (e.g., iOS 14.0).
- Set **Bundle Identifier** matching the App ID.
- Set **Version** (`CFBundleShortVersionString`) and **Build** (`CFBundleVersion`).
- Add **PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy** declaring Required Reason API usage.

#### Step 5: Archive & Upload
- In Xcode: **Product → Archive** (release configuration, Any iOS Device).
- Once Organizer opens, click **Distribute App → App Store Connect → Upload**.
- Wait for processing (typically 5–30 minutes).

#### Step 6: Submit via App Store Connect
- Log in to **https://appstoreconnect.apple.com**.
- Create a new app record.
- Fill in **App Information** (name, subtitle, category, privacy policy URL).
- Upload **screenshots** (6.7", 6.5", 5.5", 12.9" iPad), **app preview** (optional).
- Select the uploaded build.
- Answer **App Privacy** questionnaire.
- Click **Add for Review → Submit to App Review**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Apple Review Time:** 24–48 hours for first submission, can extend to 7+ days for complex apps. Rejections are common — read **App Store Review Guidelines** (Section 2: Performance, 4: Design, 5: Legal) before resubmitting.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Google Play | Apple App Store | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Cost | $25 once | $99/year | Recurring budget for iOS |
| Build Format | AAB | IPA | Android is more flexible (dynamic delivery) |
| Signing | 3-tier (upload/app/signing) | 2-tier (cert + profile) | Play model is more secure by default |
| Review Time | Hours to 7 days | 24h to 7+ days | Apple is stricter |
| Update Flexibility | High (hot updates via Play) | Low (must be reviewed) | Affects CI/CD strategy |
| Fragmentation | Many devices/configs | Limited devices | Test on emulators + real devices |
| Monetization | Wide (ads, IAP) | Wide + IAP only allowed | Apple restricts crypto/NFTs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal App Submission Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Developed App] --> B[Generate Release Build]
    B --> C[Code Signing & Obfuscation]
    C --> D[Prepare Store Assets]
    D --> E[Upload to Play Console]
    D --> F[Upload to App Store Connect]
    E --> G[Google Automated Review]
    F --> H[Apple Human Review]
    G --> I{Approved?}
    H --> J{Approved?}
    I -- Yes --> K[Staged Rollout 1% to 100%]
    I -- No --> L[Fix Issues and Resubmit]
    J -- Yes --> M[Worldwide Release]
    J -- No --> N[Address Rejection and Resubmit]
    K --> O[Monitor Crashes and Analytics]
    M --> O
    L --> P[End: Re-Enter Pipeline]
    N --> P
    O --> Q[End: Live App in Production]
    P --> B
```

### 4.2 Google Play Signing Architecture (3-Tier Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dev[Developer Machine] -->|Signs AAB with| UploadKey[Upload Key]
    UploadKey -->|Uploaded to| PlayConsole[Play Console]
    PlayConsole -->|Google uses| AppSignKey[App Signing Key - Held by Google]
    AppSignKey -->|Generates per-device| APK1[APK for x86]
    AppSignKey -->|Generates per-device| APK2[APK for ARM64]
    AppSignKey -->|Generates per-device| APK3[APK for ARMv7]
    APK1 --> DeviceA[End User Device A]
    APK2 --> DeviceB[End User Device B]
    APK3 --> DeviceC[End User Device C]
```

### 4.3 Apple Code Signing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CSR[CSR Generated in Keychain] --> Apple[Apple Developer Portal]
    Apple -->|Issues| DevCert[Development Certificate]
    Apple -->|Issues| DistCert[Distribution Certificate .p12]
    DevCert --> DevProfile[Development Provisioning Profile]
    DistCert --> DistProfile[Distribution Provisioning Profile]
    DistProfile --> Xcode[Xcode Archive Build]
    Xcode -->|Signed with| IPA[IPA File]
    IPA --> AppStore[App Store Connect Upload]
    AppStore --> Review[Apple Review Team]
    Review -->|Approved| Users[End Users via App Store]
```

### 4.4 Pre-Submission Checklist Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Checklist[Pre-Submission Checklist] --> A1[App Icon Ready]
    Checklist --> A2[Screenshots for All Devices]
    Checklist --> A3[Privacy Policy URL Live]
    Checklist --> A4[Content Rating Completed]
    Checklist --> A5[Data Safety Form Filled]
    Checklist --> A6[Version Code Incremented]
    Checklist --> A7[Keystore Secured]
    Checklist --> A8[ProGuard Rules Tested]
    Checklist --> A9[Crash Reporting Integrated]
    Checklist --> A10[Tested on Real Devices]
    A1 --> Ready[Ready to Submit]
    A2 --> Ready
    A3 --> Ready
    A4 --> Ready
    A5 --> Ready
    A6 --> Ready
    A7 --> Ready
    A8 --> Ready
    A9 --> Ready
    A10 --> Ready
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List the differences between AAB and APK formats in Android. Why does Google recommend AAB for Play Store submissions?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **AAB (Android App Bundle):** A publishing format containing all compiled code and resources. Google Play uses it to generate and serve **optimized APKs per device configuration** (dynamic delivery). Reduces download size by ~15–30%. (1.5 marks)
- **APK (Android Package Kit):** A single, pre-built installable file. Cannot be customized per device. Still used for direct distribution outside Play Store. (1 mark)
- **Why AAB:** Smaller downloads, dynamic feature delivery, mandatory for new apps on Play Store since **August 2021**. (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*What is the difference between the Apple Distribution Certificate and the Apple Development Certificate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Development Certificate:** Used to sign apps for **testing on registered devices** during development. Limited to specific device UDIDs. (1.5 marks)
- **Distribution Certificate:** Used to sign apps for **App Store submission and TestFlight external testing**. Not tied to specific devices. (1.5 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **any ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for preparing and submitting an Android application to the **Google Play Store**. Cover pre-submission tasks, asset requirements, and the release process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Pre-Submission Engineering (2 marks)**
   - Create Google Play Developer account ($25 one-time).
   - Set up **App Signing**: generate upload keystore using `keytool`.
   - Configure `build.gradle` with `signingConfigs.release` and enable `minifyEnabled true` (R8/ProGuard) and `shrinkResources true`.
   - Increment `versionCode` and `versionName` in `build.gradle`.

2. **Asset & Metadata Preparation (2 marks)**
   - App icon: 512×512 px PNG.
   - Feature graphic: 1024×500 px.
   - Screenshots: minimum 2, recommended 8 per device class.
   - Short description (80 chars), full description (4000 chars).
   - Privacy policy URL hosted on a public domain.

3. **Build & Upload (1.5 marks)**
   - In Android Studio: **Build → Generate Signed Bundle → Android App Bundle**.
   - Choose `release` variant.
   - Upload `.aab` to Play Console → Release → Production.

4. **Console Configuration & Rollout (1.5 marks)**
   - Fill **Content Rating** (IARC), **Target Audience**, **Data Safety** forms.
   - Set pricing, distribution countries.
   - Click **Review release → Start rollout to Production**.
   - Optionally use **staged rollout** (1% → 10% → 50% → 100%).

---

**(b)** Discuss the importance of the **Privacy Manifest** and **Data Safety** declarations for both Google Play and Apple App Store. What happens if a developer omits these? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Apple Privacy Manifest (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`)** (3.5 marks)
   - Mandatory for all apps from **May 1, 2024**.
   - Declares **Required Reason APIs** used (e.g., UserDefaults, FileTimestamp, SystemBootTime, DiskSpace).
   - Must be bundled inside the app and submitted via App Store Connect.
   - Apple uses this for **App Privacy Report** shown to users in Settings.
   - **Omission consequence:** App is **rejected** during App Review with reference to **Guideline 5.1.1(ix)**.

2. **Google Play Data Safety Form** (2.5 marks)
   - Mandatory declaration of:
     - Data **collected** (e.g., location, contacts, personal info).
     - Data **shared** with third parties.
     - Security practices (encryption in transit, user data deletion).
   - Shown to users on the Play Store listing.
   - **Omission consequence:** App **cannot be published**; Play Console blocks the release track.

3. **Engineering Importance** (1 mark)
   - Both stores align with global regulations: **GDPR (EU)**, **DPDPA (India 2023)**, **CCPA (California)**.
   - Builds user trust and reduces legal risk.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the complete procedure for submitting an iOS app to the **Apple App Store**, including the role of **Xcode**, **App Store Connect**, and the **TestFlight** beta testing platform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Enrollment & Setup (1.5 marks)**
   - Join **Apple Developer Program** ($99/year).
   - Register **App ID** in Developer Portal (reverse-DNS like `com.ktu.labapp`).
   - Generate **Distribution Certificate** via Keychain Access → CSR → Apple Portal.

2. **Xcode Configuration (1.5 marks)**
   - Set **Deployment Target** (e.g., iOS 14.0).
   - Set **Bundle Identifier** matching App ID.
   - Configure **Signing & Capabilities** tab with the distribution profile.
   - Increment **Version** and **Build** numbers.

3. **Archive & Upload (2 marks)**
   - Select **Any iOS Device** as target.
   - **Product → Archive** with Release configuration.
   - In Organizer: **Distribute App → App Store Connect → Upload**.
   - Wait for Apple to process the build (~5–30 minutes).

4. **App Store Connect Submission (1 mark)**
   - Create new app record.
   - Fill metadata: name, subtitle, category, privacy URL.
   - Upload screenshots (6.7", 6.5", 12.9" iPad).
   - Select uploaded build, complete App Privacy questionnaire.
   - Click **Add for Review → Submit to App Review**.

5. **TestFlight Beta (1 mark)**
   - Pre-release: invite up to **100 internal testers** (App Store Connect users) and **10,000 external testers** via email or public link.
   - Builds require **Beta App Review** (lighter than full review).
   - Crash reports and feedback collected via TestFlight app.

---

**(b)** Compare the **app review process** of Google Play and Apple App Store in terms of strictness, time, and common rejection reasons. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Google Play (3.5 marks) | Apple App Store (3.5 marks) |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Strictness | Automated checks + spot manual review | **Strict human review** by Apple team |
| Time | Hours to 7 days | 24 hours to 7+ days (often longer) |
| Common Rejections | Policy violations, malware, metadata mismatch, deceptive ads | Guideline 2 (crashes), 4 (design flaws), 5.1.1 (privacy), 3.1.1 (in-app purchase) |
| Appeal Process | Limited, via Play Console support | **App Review Board** appeal available |
| Pre-Release Testing | Internal, Closed, Open tracks | TestFlight Internal + External |

**Engineering Takeaway:** Developers targeting both stores should expect **Apple rejections to be the bottleneck**. Adopt a **CI/CD pipeline with Fastlane** to automate builds, signing, and metadata submission for both stores efficiently.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning**
> - Students often **confuse APK with AAB** — remember, AAB is for Play Store, APK is for direct/sideloading.
> - Many forget the **$25 vs $99 fee distinction** — examiners specifically test this.
> - Do NOT skip mentioning **versionCode/versionName** OR **CFBundleVersion/CFBundleShortVersionString** — these are guaranteed 1–2 marks.
> - Forgetting to mention **staged rollout** or **phased release** loses 1 mark easily.
> - Writing "submit app" without listing **screenshots, icon, privacy policy** is treated as an **incomplete answer** in KTU valuation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AAB** is mandatory for new Play Store apps since August 2021; **IPA** is the iOS equivalent.
- **Google Play** charges **$25 one-time**; **Apple** charges **$99/year**.
- **Code signing** is non-negotiable: upload key + app signing key (Google), or distribution certificate + provisioning profile (Apple).
- **Privacy Manifest (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`)** is mandatory on Apple from **May 2024**; **Data Safety form** is mandatory on Google Play.
- Always **increment `versionCode`/`CFBundleVersion`** before every release or the upload will be rejected.
- **ProGuard/R8** (`minifyEnabled true`) reduces APK size and obfuscates code — required for production.
- **Staged rollout** on Google Play (1% → 100%) minimizes risk; Apple offers **phased release** (7-day automatic).
- **TestFlight** supports up to 100 internal + 10,000 external testers; Google Play has **Internal, Closed, Open** tracks.
- **Common Apple rejection**: Guideline 4 (Design), 5.1.1 (Privacy); **Common Google rejection**: deceptive metadata, unsafe content.
- **CI/CD tools**: **Fastlane**, **Bitrise**, **GitHub Actions** are industry-standard for automating submissions.
- Always test on **real devices**, not just emulators, before final submission.
- Keep **keystores and certificates** backed up securely — losing them means losing the ability to update the app.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
